title: Improved error estimates of a new splitting scheme for charged-particle dynamics in strong magnetic field with maximal ordering
summary: This paper introduces a novel second-order splitting scheme for charged-particle dynamics in strong magnetic fields characterized by the maximal ordering. The proposed scheme is explicit and symmetric, which respectively ensure the efficiency of the algorithm and its long-term near-conservation of energy. We rigorously prove that the scheme achieves improved error bounds for both the position and the velocity component parallel to the magnetic field, yielding a uniform second-order error bound under specific strong-field regimes. Numerical experiments confirm the optimal convergence rates and the long-term energy near conservation of the method.

AI.tldr: Novel explicit symmetric second-order splitting scheme for charged-particle dynamics in strong magnetic fields with improved error bounds and long-term energy conservation.
AI.motivation: Need efficient and accurate numerical methods for simulating charged-particle dynamics in strong magnetic fields, particularly addressing challenges of maximal ordering regimes where conventional methods may fail.
AI.method: Developed a second-order splitting scheme that is explicit (for computational efficiency) and symmetric (for structure preservation). The method specifically handles the maximal ordering regime of strong magnetic fields.
AI.result: Proved rigorous error bounds showing uniform second-order accuracy for position and parallel velocity components under strong-field conditions. Numerical experiments confirm optimal convergence rates and demonstrate long-term near-conservation of energy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scheme provides an efficient, accurate, and structure-preserving numerical method for charged-particle dynamics in strong magnetic fields, with proven theoretical guarantees and practical performance.

title: Oblivious Subspace Injection Is Not Enough for Relative Error
summary: Oblivious subspace injection (OSI) was introduced by Camaño, Epperly, Meyer, and Tropp in 2025 as a much weaker sketching property than oblivious subspace embedding (OSE) that still yields constant-factor guarantees for randomized low-rank approximation and sketch-and-solve least-squares regression. At the Simons Institute in Berkeley during a workshop in October 2025, it was asked whether OSIs also imply relative error bounds rather than just constant-factor guarantees. We show that, from a theoretical standpoint, OSI alone does not yield OSE-style relative-error guarantees whose failure probability is controlled solely by the OSI failure parameter, even though OSI sketches often perform extremely well in practice. We provide counterexamples showing this for sketch-and-solve least squares and for randomized SVD in the Frobenius norm. The missing ingredient from a sketch satisfying only OSI is upper control on the optimal residual or tail component, and when one ensures the sketch has this additional property, a near-relative-error bound is recovered. We also show that there is a natural $\ell_p$ analogue of OSI giving constant-factor sketch-and-solve bounds.

AI.tldr: OSI (oblivious subspace injection) doesn't guarantee relative-error bounds despite working well in practice; counterexamples show this for sketch-and-solve least squares and randomized SVD; adding upper control on residuals recovers near-relative-error bounds.
AI.motivation: To determine whether OSI (a weaker sketching property than OSE) can provide relative-error guarantees, not just constant-factor guarantees, as asked at a Simons Institute workshop.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis showing OSI alone doesn't guarantee relative-error bounds, providing counterexamples for sketch-and-solve least squares and randomized SVD in Frobenius norm. Identifies missing ingredient (upper control on optimal residual) and shows adding it recovers near-relative-error bounds.
AI.result: OSI alone doesn't yield OSE-style relative-error guarantees; counterexamples demonstrate this limitation; adding upper control on residuals enables near-relative-error bounds; also shows natural ℓ_p analogue of OSI gives constant-factor sketch-and-solve bounds.
AI.conclusion: While OSI provides constant-factor guarantees and works well practically, it lacks theoretical guarantees for relative-error bounds without additional control on residuals; this clarifies the theoretical limitations of OSI compared to OSE.

title: Nonconforming $hp$-FE/BE coupling on unstructured meshes based on Nitsche's method
comment: 36 pages, 7 figures, submitted to Numerische Mathematik
summary: We construct and analyse a $hp$-FE/BE coupling on non-matching meshes, based on Nitsche's method. Both the mesh size and the polynomial degree are changed to improve accuracy. Nitsche's method leads to a positive definite formulation, thus, unlike the mortar method, it does not require the Babuška-Brezzi condition for stability. The method is stable provided the stabilization function is larger than a certain threshold. We obtain an explicit bound for the threshold and derive a priori error estimates. Our analysis can be easily extended to the pure FE or the pure BE decomposition as well as to the case of more than two subdomains. The problem in a bounded domain is considered in detail, but the case of an unbounded BE subdomain and a bounded FE subdomain follows with similar arguments. We develop convergence analysis and provide numerical examples for quasi-uniform as well as geometrically refined $hp$ discretisations in both subdomains with analytic and singular solutions.

AI.tldr: A $hp$-FE/BE coupling method on non-matching meshes using Nitsche's method, with both mesh size and polynomial degree adaptation for improved accuracy.
AI.motivation: To develop a stable coupling method for finite element (FE) and boundary element (BE) discretizations on non-matching meshes without requiring the Babuška-Brezzi condition for stability, unlike mortar methods.
AI.method: Nitsche's method is used to couple FE and BE domains on non-matching meshes with $hp$-adaptivity (both mesh size h and polynomial degree p). The method provides a positive definite formulation with stability ensured when the stabilization function exceeds a certain threshold.
AI.result: Explicit bound for the stabilization threshold is obtained, a priori error estimates are derived, and the method is shown to be stable. The analysis extends to pure FE/BE decompositions, multiple subdomains, and both bounded/unbounded domains. Numerical examples demonstrate convergence for various discretizations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed $hp$-FE/BE coupling via Nitsche's method provides a stable, positive definite formulation for non-matching meshes without requiring the Babuška-Brezzi condition, with explicit stability bounds and proven convergence properties.

title: A High-Order Conformal FEM for Multidimensional Nonlinear Collisional Breakage Equations: Analysis and Computation
summary: Particle breakage due to collisional interactions plays a vital role in the development of several phenomena in science and engineering. The nonlinear collisional breakage equations (NCBEs) are a significant set of equations in this context. Solving the NCBE is computationally challenging due to its nonlinearity, high dimensionality, and complex kernel interactions. Solving NCBE problems is more complex in two- and three-dimensional problems. In these problems, it is more challenging to evaluate multidimensional moments and integrals, maintain solution stability, and achieve computational efficiency. Despite the importance of the NCBE in science and engineering, the development of efficient numerical methods for solving it in two- and three-dimensional problems has not been adequately explored. In this work, we have introduced a new framework for solving the NCBE across multiple dimensions using the conformal finite element method (FEM). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to solve the NCBE using the conformal FEM. The new framework employs high-order Lagrange elements in conjunction with the BDF2 scheme for time discretization. The present method preserves the important physical quantities such as the total count and hypervolume of the population particles. Convergence results for error estimates have also been derived for both semidiscrete and fully discrete schemes. Numerical experiments have been carried out for one-, two-, and three-dimensional problems. The numerical experiments have shown that the proposed method achieved high accuracy, optimal convergence rates, and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: A new conformal finite element method framework for solving multidimensional nonlinear collisional breakage equations with high accuracy and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Particle breakage due to collisions is important in science/engineering, but solving the nonlinear collisional breakage equations (NCBEs) is computationally challenging in 2D/3D due to nonlinearity, high dimensionality, complex kernel interactions, and difficulties evaluating multidimensional moments/integrals. Existing methods for multidimensional NCBEs are insufficiently explored.
AI.method: A novel framework using conformal finite element method (FEM) with high-order Lagrange elements for spatial discretization and BDF2 scheme for time discretization. This is the first application of conformal FEM to NCBEs.
AI.result: The method preserves important physical quantities (total count and hypervolume of particles), achieves high accuracy, optimal convergence rates, and computational efficiency. Convergence error estimates derived for both semidiscrete and fully discrete schemes. Numerical experiments validated performance for 1D, 2D, and 3D problems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed conformal FEM framework successfully addresses the computational challenges of solving NCBEs in multiple dimensions, providing an efficient and accurate numerical method that preserves physical properties and achieves optimal convergence.

title: Firewall effect on charged particle acceleration by circularly polarized waves and parallel electric fields
summary: We report an unanticipated charged-particle dynamics in a classical setting of a uniform magnetic field, a parallel electric field, and a circularly polarized wave. The setting admits a natural trajectory that a particle accelerated by the electric field reaches a Doppler-shifted cyclotron resonance and becomes trapped in the resonance space. Remarkably, once trapped, the particle experiences acceleration opposite to its prior motion simultaneously with perpendicular acceleration, despite the constant direction of the parallel electric field. This counterintuitive behavior has important implications for particle scattering in various laboratory and space plasmas. Applied to fusion devices, particle-in-cell simulations show that an externally injected R-wave can act as a firewall suppressing further runaway-electron acceleration.

AI.tldr: Charged particles in magnetic/electric fields with circularly polarized waves can become trapped in Doppler-shifted cyclotron resonance, leading to counterintuitive acceleration opposite to prior motion despite constant electric field direction.
AI.motivation: To understand unexpected charged-particle dynamics in combined magnetic fields, electric fields, and circularly polarized waves, which has implications for particle scattering in laboratory and space plasmas.
AI.method: Analysis of classical setting with uniform magnetic field, parallel electric field, and circularly polarized wave; study of natural trajectory where particles accelerated by electric field reach Doppler-shifted cyclotron resonance and become trapped; particle-in-cell simulations for fusion device applications.
AI.result: Particles trapped in resonance space experience acceleration opposite to their prior motion simultaneously with perpendicular acceleration, despite constant parallel electric field direction; externally injected R-wave can act as firewall suppressing runaway-electron acceleration in fusion devices.
AI.conclusion: The counterintuitive particle dynamics has important implications for plasma physics, particularly for controlling runaway electrons in fusion devices through wave injection as a suppression mechanism.

title: Corrigendum to "Optimal time-decay estimates for an Oldroyd-B model with zero viscosity [J. Differential Equations, 306(2022), 456--491]"
summary: The present note is to make minor correction on the assumption of Theorem 1.2 and its proof in our paper [arXiv:2111.02059, Jinrui Huang, Yinghui Wang, Huanyao Wen and Rizhao Zi, {\it J. Differential Equations}, 306(2022), 456--491].

AI.tldr: Correction note for Theorem 1.2 in previous paper, fixing minor assumption and proof issues.
AI.motivation: To correct errors in the original paper's Theorem 1.2 and its proof that were identified after publication.
AI.method: Provides specific corrections to the mathematical assumptions and proof steps of Theorem 1.2 from the original paper.
AI.result: Corrected version of Theorem 1.2 with proper assumptions and proof, maintaining the paper's main conclusions.
AI.conclusion: The correction addresses minor but important mathematical issues, ensuring the theorem's validity while preserving the original paper's core results.

title: Learning noisy phase transition dynamics from stochastic partial differential equations
summary: The non-equilibrium dynamics of mesoscale phase transitions are fundamentally shaped by thermal fluctuations, which not only seed instabilities but actively control kinetic pathways, including rare barrier-crossing events such as nucleation that are entirely inaccessible to deterministic models. Machine-learning surrogates for such systems must therefore represent stochasticity explicitly, enforce conservation laws by construction, and expose physically interpretable structure. We develop physics-aware surrogate models for the stochastic Cahn-Hilliard equation in 3D that satisfy all three requirements simultaneously. The key innovation is to parameterize the surrogate at the level of inter-cell fluxes, decomposing each flux into a deterministic mobility-weighted chemical-potential gradient and a learnable noise amplitude. This design guarantees exact mass conservation at every step and adds physical fluctuations to inter-cell mass transport. A learnable free energy functional provides thermodynamic interpretability, validated by independent recovery of the bulk double-well landscape, interfacial excess energy, and curvature-independent interfacial tension. Tests demonstrate accurate reproduction of ensemble statistics and noise-accelerated coarsening, with generalization to spatial domains 64 times larger in volume and temporal horizons 160x longer than those seen during training. Critically, the stochastic surrogate captures thermally activated nucleation in the metastable regime, a qualitative capability that no deterministic surrogate can provide regardless of training, thus establishing flux-level stochasticity as an architectural necessity rather than an optional enhancement.

AI.tldr: Physics-aware ML surrogate for 3D stochastic Cahn-Hilliard equation that enforces mass conservation, includes learnable noise for thermal fluctuations, and captures rare nucleation events.
AI.motivation: Traditional deterministic models fail to capture thermal fluctuations that control kinetic pathways and rare events like nucleation in mesoscale phase transitions. ML surrogates need to represent stochasticity explicitly while enforcing conservation laws and maintaining physical interpretability.
AI.method: Parameterize surrogate at inter-cell flux level, decomposing each flux into deterministic mobility-weighted chemical-potential gradient and learnable noise amplitude. Includes learnable free energy functional for thermodynamic interpretability, guaranteeing exact mass conservation at every step.
AI.result: Accurately reproduces ensemble statistics and noise-accelerated coarsening. Generalizes to spatial domains 64× larger and temporal horizons 160× longer than training data. Captures thermally activated nucleation in metastable regime - a qualitative capability impossible for deterministic surrogates.
AI.conclusion: Flux-level stochasticity is an architectural necessity, not optional enhancement, for ML surrogates of stochastic phase field models. The approach provides physically interpretable structure while capturing rare events inaccessible to deterministic models.

title: A DPG method for the circular arch problem
summary: We consider an elastic model for a circular arch that incorporates membrane, transverse shear, and bending effects. The central line of the arch is partitioned into elements, and an ultra-weak variational formulation is developed alongside a discontinuous Petrov-Galerkin (DPG) approximation procedure based on so-called optimal test functions. The formulation uses discontinuous stress and displacement interpolations on the element mesh, with corresponding interface variables defined at the nodes. Theoretical analysis predicts optimal convergence rates for all quantities of interest, while also revealing potential error amplification influenced by the curvature of the arch and the imposed boundary conditions. The method is tested on examples with different support configurations. The numerical experiments confirm the theoretical predictions and further demonstrate that the accuracy of the DPG method can be improved by employing a suitably scaled test space norm.

AI.tldr: DPG method applied to elastic circular arch model with membrane, shear, and bending effects, using discontinuous interpolations and optimal test functions for improved accuracy.
AI.motivation: To develop an accurate numerical method for analyzing elastic circular arches that incorporates all relevant mechanical effects (membrane, transverse shear, bending) while achieving optimal convergence rates.
AI.method: Discontinuous Petrov-Galerkin (DPG) approximation with ultra-weak variational formulation, using discontinuous stress and displacement interpolations on element mesh with interface variables at nodes, employing optimal test functions.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis predicts optimal convergence rates, but reveals potential error amplification influenced by arch curvature and boundary conditions. Numerical experiments confirm predictions and show accuracy can be improved with suitably scaled test space norm.
AI.conclusion: DPG method is effective for elastic arch analysis, achieving optimal convergence rates with demonstrated accuracy improvements through proper test space norm scaling, though performance depends on curvature and boundary conditions.

title: Ion shielding effects on the resonant boundary layer response to magnetic perturbations
summary: Fusion plasmas are highly sensitive to external magnetic perturbations which result in complex responses near a region known as the resonant layer. Past analytic descriptions of this phenomena used boundary layer theory in a simplified system assuming low plasma beta to predict the onset of instabilities. Here, we present a novel extension of the analytic theory utilizing nested boundary layers to capture the physics of ion parallel flow at the plasma resonant layer. This new prediction supports previous numerical results and suggests ion shielding against magnetic disruptions in parameter regimes relevant to future device operation.

AI.tldr: Extended analytic theory using nested boundary layers to model ion parallel flow at plasma resonant layer, supporting numerical results and suggesting ion shielding against magnetic disruptions.
AI.motivation: Fusion plasmas are highly sensitive to external magnetic perturbations near resonant layers, and past analytic descriptions using boundary layer theory were limited to simplified systems with low plasma beta assumptions.
AI.method: Developed a novel extension of analytic theory using nested boundary layers to capture the physics of ion parallel flow at the plasma resonant layer.
AI.result: The new analytic prediction supports previous numerical results and suggests ion shielding against magnetic disruptions in parameter regimes relevant to future fusion device operation.
AI.conclusion: The nested boundary layer approach provides improved analytic understanding of plasma responses to magnetic perturbations, with implications for stability and disruption mitigation in future fusion devices.

title: A mathematical model for colloids deposition in porous media combined with a moving boundary at the microscale: Solvability and numerical simulation
summary:
We study a reaction-diffusion model posed on two distinct spatial scales that accounts for diffusion, aggregation, fragmentation, and deposition of populations of colloidal particles within a porous material. In this model, the macroscopic transport of the particles is described by an effective equation whose transport coefficients are determined by cell problems posed on the underlying pore scale.
  The internal pore geometry can change over time due to deposition or detachment of colloidal particles. We represent the evolving microstructure as solid cores whose phase boundaries can grow or shrink over time. As deposition progresses, neighbouring growing cores may come into contact, leading to local clogging of the pore space. We investigate how such evolving microstructures influence the effective transport and storage properties of porous layers. We establish basic analytical results concerning the weak solvability of the resulting multiscale evolution problem, which takes the form of a strongly non-linear parabolic system, in the non-clogging regime. For the numerical approximation of weak solutions we propose a two-scale finite element discretization. Numerical experiments illustrate how local clogging affects the effective dispersion tensor and quantify the resulting trade-off between transport efficiency and storage capacity.

AI.tldr: Multiscale reaction-diffusion model for colloidal transport in porous media with evolving microstructure due to particle deposition/detachment, including clogging effects and numerical analysis.
AI.motivation: To understand how evolving microstructures in porous materials (due to colloidal particle deposition/detachment) affect effective transport and storage properties, particularly when clogging occurs.
AI.method: Two-scale mathematical model with macroscopic transport equations whose coefficients are determined by pore-scale cell problems; analytical study of weak solvability in non-clogging regime; two-scale finite element discretization for numerical approximation.
AI.result: Established basic analytical results for weak solvability of the strongly nonlinear parabolic system; developed numerical method; showed how local clogging affects effective dispersion tensor and creates trade-off between transport efficiency and storage capacity.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a framework for analyzing multiscale colloidal transport in porous media with evolving microstructure, offering insights into clogging effects on transport properties and the efficiency-storage trade-off.

title: Differentiable free energy surface: a variational approach to directly observing rare events using generative deep-learning models
comment: Main text: 20 pages, 5 figures. Supplement: 12 pages
summary: Rare events are central to the evolution of complex many-body systems, characterized as key transitional configurations on the free energy surface (FES). Conventional methods require adequate sampling of rare event transitions to obtain the FES, which is computationally very demanding. Here we introduce the variational free energy surface (VaFES), a dataset-free framework that directly models FESs using tractable-density generative models. Rare events can then be immediately identified from the FES with their configurations generated directly via one-shot sampling of generative models. By extending a coarse-grained collective variable (CV) into its reversible equivalent, VaFES constructs a latent space of intermediate representation in which the CVs explicitly occupy a subset of dimensions. This latent-space construction preserves the physical interpretability and transparent controllability of the CVs by design, while accommodating arbitrary CV formulations. The reversibility makes the system energy exactly accessible, enabling variational optimization of the FES without pre-generated simulation data. A single optimization yields a continuous, differentiable FES together with one-shot generation of rare-event configurations. Our method can reproduce the exact analytical solution for the bistable dimer potential and identify a chignolin native folded state in close alignment with the experimental NMR structure. Our approach thus establishes a scalable, systematic framework for advancing the study of complex statistical systems.

AI.tldr: VaFES is a dataset-free framework that directly models free energy surfaces using generative models, enabling one-shot sampling of rare-event configurations without extensive simulation data.
AI.motivation: Conventional methods for studying rare events in complex systems require extensive sampling of transitions to obtain free energy surfaces, which is computationally demanding. There's a need for more efficient approaches to identify and generate rare-event configurations.
AI.method: Introduces variational free energy surface (VaFES) framework that uses tractable-density generative models. Extends coarse-grained collective variables into reversible equivalents to construct a latent space where CVs occupy explicit dimensions. This preserves interpretability while enabling variational optimization of FES without pre-generated simulation data.
AI.result: Method reproduces exact analytical solution for bistable dimer potential and identifies chignolin native folded state closely aligned with experimental NMR structure. Single optimization yields continuous, differentiable FES with one-shot generation of rare-event configurations.
AI.conclusion: VaFES establishes a scalable, systematic framework for advancing the study of complex statistical systems by enabling direct modeling of free energy surfaces and efficient identification/generation of rare-event configurations without extensive computational sampling.

title: Asymptotic and pre-asymptotic convergence of sparse grids for anisotropic kernel interpolation
summary: Sparse grids are popular tools for high-dimensional function approximation. In this work, we study the use of sparse grids for interpolation using separable Matérn kernels $Φ_{\boldsymbolν,\boldsymbolλ}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{x}')=\prod_{j=1}^dφ_{ν_j,λ_j}(x_j,x_j')$, with a particular focus on the anisotropic setting where the regularity $ν_j$ and the lengthscale $λ_j$ vary with dimension $j$. We combine the construction of anisotropic sparse grids, which exploit anisotropic $ν_j$ to improve convergence rates in smooth dimensions, with the construction of lengthscale-informed sparse grids, which diminish the error contribution of less varying dimensions using anisotropic $λ_j$. We provide theory and numerical experiments to showcase the benefits on asymptotic and pre-asymptotic error behaviour of sparse grid kernel interpolation.

AI.tldr: Sparse grids combined with anisotropic Matérn kernels improve high-dimensional interpolation by exploiting varying smoothness and lengthscales across dimensions.
AI.motivation: Sparse grids are effective for high-dimensional approximation, but can be enhanced by incorporating anisotropic kernel properties (varying smoothness and lengthscales) to better handle dimensions with different characteristics.
AI.method: Combine anisotropic sparse grids (exploiting varying smoothness ν_j) with lengthscale-informed sparse grids (using varying lengthscales λ_j) for Matérn kernel interpolation, with theoretical analysis and numerical experiments.
AI.result: The combined approach improves both asymptotic and pre-asymptotic error behavior of sparse grid kernel interpolation by better handling anisotropic dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating anisotropic sparse grids with lengthscale-informed construction significantly enhances sparse grid kernel interpolation performance for high-dimensional problems with varying dimensional characteristics.

title: 3D Kinetic Simulations of Driven Reconnection in Merging Flux Tubes
summary: We present 2D and 3D Particle-in-Cell simulations of driven collisionless magnetic reconnection triggered by the compression and merger of two Lundquist-type force-free flux tubes in a strongly magnetized pair plasma, with a focus on magnetic energy dissipation and particle acceleration. We show that 3D effects systematically delay the onset of reconnection in comparison with equivalent 2D runs, an effect further enhanced by a strong guide field, due to reduced linear growth rates and phase decoherence of oblique modes. Increasing the external drive accelerates both tearing and drift-kink instabilities, while a strong guide field suppresses coherent drift-kink activity and has a comparatively mild impact on tearing. Despite these differences in early-time dynamics, all simulations enter a fast-merging phase characterized by a normalized reconnection rate 0.08--0.10, coinciding with a transient reduction of the guide-to-reconnecting field ratio inside the current sheet. The high-energy cutoff of accelerated particles converges to a common asymptotic value, gamma_cut/sigma_in ~ 50, with only a weak dependence on the driving strength. This behavior is consistent with an electric-field-limited acceleration process, in which the maximum energy is set by the reconnection electric field and the duration of the energization phase. The resulting nonthermal particle spectra are similar across all runs, with power-law indices p ~ 1.6--2.0.

AI.tldr: 3D PIC simulations show delayed reconnection onset vs 2D due to guide field effects, but all cases converge to similar fast reconnection rates (~0.08-0.10) and particle acceleration with gamma_cut/sigma_in ~ 50 and power-law indices p ~ 1.6-2.0.
AI.motivation: To understand how 3D effects and guide fields influence collisionless magnetic reconnection dynamics, magnetic energy dissipation, and particle acceleration in strongly magnetized pair plasmas during flux tube compression and merger.
AI.method: 2D and 3D Particle-in-Cell (PIC) simulations of driven collisionless magnetic reconnection triggered by compression and merger of two Lundquist-type force-free flux tubes in strongly magnetized pair plasma.
AI.result: 3D effects delay reconnection onset compared to 2D, enhanced by strong guide fields. Despite early differences, all simulations enter fast-merging phase with normalized reconnection rate 0.08-0.10. Particle acceleration shows consistent high-energy cutoff gamma_cut/sigma_in ~ 50 and power-law indices p ~ 1.6-2.0 across all runs.
AI.conclusion: 3D effects and guide fields modify early reconnection dynamics but don't fundamentally change the asymptotic particle acceleration process, which appears to be electric-field-limited with maximum energy set by reconnection electric field and energization duration.

title: Transition Time for Weak Singularities of the Navier-Stokes Equations
summary: This paper constructs a rigorous mathematical framework for investigating laminar-turbulent transition induced by weak singularities of incompressible Navier-Stokes (NS) equations. By integrating the energy identity of Leray weak solutions with the singularity criterion $\left\lVert \boldsymbol{u} \right\rVert_{H_0^1(Ω)}\to0$, a closed analytical form of the laminar-turbulent transition characteristic time is derived. The theoretical scaling $t_{\text{trans}}\simν/U^2$ (equivalent to $t_{\text{trans}}\sim t_c/\text{Re}$) is verified to be consistent with classical experimental observations in shear flows. This work reveals that laminar-turbulent transition is dominated by the local regularity collapse of Leray weak solutions rather than global viscous diffusion, and provides a novel theoretical interpretation for the onset of turbulence from the perspective of NS equation weak singularities.

AI.tldr: Mathematical framework links laminar-turbulent transition to weak singularities in Navier-Stokes equations, deriving transition time scaling t_trans ∼ ν/U² that matches experimental observations.
AI.motivation: To establish a rigorous mathematical connection between laminar-turbulent transition and weak singularities in incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, providing theoretical foundation for turbulence onset.
AI.method: Integrates energy identity of Leray weak solutions with singularity criterion ||u||_{H₀¹(Ω)}→0 to derive closed analytical form for transition characteristic time.
AI.result: Derived theoretical scaling t_trans ∼ ν/U² (equivalent to t_trans ∼ t_c/Re) consistent with classical experimental observations in shear flows.
AI.conclusion: Laminar-turbulent transition dominated by local regularity collapse of Leray weak solutions rather than global viscous diffusion, providing novel theoretical interpretation of turbulence onset from NS equation weak singularities.

title: Admissible Reconstruction of Reaction-Channel Levels on Fixed Subgroup Support for Cross-Section-Space Probability Table Constructions
summary: In cross-section-space probability table constructions, reaction-channel levels are reconstructed on fixed total-subgroup nodes and probabilities. Although the standard full-matching reconstruction is uniquely determined, it does not in general preserve componentwise nonnegativity of the channel levels. We impose nonnegativity both for physical interpretability and because, on fixed positive total-subgroup nodes and probabilities, it provides a sufficient structural condition for nonnegativity of the folded effective cross section over all dilutions. We therefore formulate an admissible constrained reconstruction problem on the fixed subgroup support, in which selected low-order channel information is retained exactly and the remaining matching conditions are fitted in a weighted least-squares sense. After null-space reduction, the problem becomes a convex optimization problem with linear inequality constraints. For the single-retention formulation, nonnegative feasibility is automatic when the retained \(0\)-order aggregate is nonnegative, whereas for a two-retention variant it additionally requires a compatibility condition with the fixed total-subgroup nodes. Numerical results for a representative U-238 capture benchmark show that nonnegativity violations are confined to a small subset of energy groups. On these groups, the admissible reconstruction restores nonnegativity, but at the cost of some response-level deterioration relative to full matching. In the comparison, the single-retention formulation shows the more stable overall behavior.

AI.tldr: Nonnegative constrained reconstruction for cross-section probability tables to preserve physical interpretability and ensure nonnegativity of folded effective cross sections across all dilutions.
AI.motivation: Standard full-matching reconstruction in cross-section-space probability tables doesn't preserve componentwise nonnegativity of channel levels, which is needed for physical interpretability and provides a sufficient structural condition for nonnegativity of folded effective cross sections over all dilutions.
AI.method: Formulate an admissible constrained reconstruction problem on fixed subgroup support: retain selected low-order channel information exactly, fit remaining matching conditions in weighted least-squares sense. After null-space reduction, problem becomes convex optimization with linear inequality constraints. Two variants: single-retention (0-order aggregate) and two-retention (additional compatibility condition).
AI.result: Numerical results for U-238 capture benchmark show nonnegativity violations confined to small subset of energy groups. Admissible reconstruction restores nonnegativity but with some response-level deterioration relative to full matching. Single-retention formulation shows more stable overall behavior.
AI.conclusion: Constrained reconstruction successfully addresses nonnegativity issues in probability table construction, with single-retention formulation providing stable performance despite some trade-off in response-level accuracy.

title: Convergence Analysis of the Hessian Discretisation Method for Fourth Order Semi-linear Elliptic Equations with General Source
summary: This paper presents a convergence analysis for the Hessian Discretisation Method (HDM) applied to fourth-order semilinear elliptic equations involving a trilinear nonlinearity and general source, based on two complementary approaches. The HDM serves as a unified framework for the convergence analysis of various numerical schemes, including conforming and nonconforming finite element methods (ncFEMs) and gradient recovery (GR) based methods. Error estimates for the Adini ncFEM and GR methods are derived for the first time, which provide an explicit order of convergence. The analysis relies on four key HDM properties along with a suitable companion operator to establish convergence results. Moreover, a convergence analysis is developed within the HDM framework, which does not require additional regularity assumptions on the exact solution or the assumption that the exact solution is regular. The paper further discusses two significant applications: the Navier--Stokes equations in stream function--vorticity formulation and the von Kármán equations for plate bending. Numerical experiments are provided to demonstrate the performance of the GR method, Morley, and Adini ncFEMs.

AI.tldr: Convergence analysis of Hessian Discretisation Method for fourth-order semilinear elliptic equations with trilinear nonlinearity, providing unified framework for various numerical schemes including conforming/nonconforming FEMs and gradient recovery methods.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified convergence analysis framework for fourth-order semilinear elliptic equations with trilinear nonlinearity that can handle various numerical schemes without requiring additional regularity assumptions on exact solutions.
AI.method: Uses Hessian Discretisation Method (HDM) with four key properties and suitable companion operator; applies two complementary approaches for convergence analysis; includes analysis for Adini ncFEM and gradient recovery methods.
AI.result: First-time derivation of error estimates with explicit convergence order for Adini ncFEM and GR methods; convergence analysis without additional regularity assumptions; applications to Navier-Stokes and von Kármán equations; numerical experiments demonstrating performance.
AI.conclusion: HDM provides effective unified framework for convergence analysis of various numerical schemes for fourth-order semilinear elliptic equations, with practical applications to important physical problems and validated by numerical experiments.

title: FIREFLY: heat load and particle exhaust approximations for rapid evaluation of divertor designs
summary: The divertor in a magnetic confinement fusion reactor is an essential component for power dissipation and particle removal. The FIREFLY package for rapid evaluation of divertor designs is presented as an extension of the FLARE code for field line reconstruction from a flux tube mesh. First, divertor loads are approximated with a simplified heat transport model. Neutralized particles are then sampled from the resulting load distribution, and the EIRENE code is used to track molecules and atoms in a plasma background while accounting for dissociation, charge exchange and ionization. Particles are removed on pumping surfaces in order to estimate the exhaust efficiency for a given divertor geometry. Optimization of the divertor geometry for more efficient particle exhaust is explored by using W7-X as an example, and the sensitivity to model parameters for the plasma background in the proxy calculations is evaluated.

AI.tldr: FIREFLY package extends FLARE code for rapid divertor design evaluation using simplified heat transport and EIRENE particle tracking to optimize exhaust efficiency.
AI.motivation: Divertors are critical components in fusion reactors for power dissipation and particle removal, but designing them efficiently requires rapid evaluation tools that can assess exhaust performance for different geometries.
AI.method: Extends FLARE code's field line reconstruction with simplified heat transport model to approximate divertor loads, then samples neutralized particles and tracks them using EIRENE code while accounting for dissociation, charge exchange, and ionization. Particles are removed on pumping surfaces to estimate exhaust efficiency.
AI.result: Developed FIREFLY package for rapid divertor design evaluation, demonstrated optimization of divertor geometry for more efficient particle exhaust using W7-X as an example, and evaluated sensitivity to model parameters for plasma background.
AI.conclusion: FIREFLY provides a practical tool for rapid divertor design assessment and optimization, enabling more efficient particle exhaust through geometry optimization while accounting for key plasma-neutral interactions.

title: On some 1D nonlocal models with coefficients changing sign
summary: In this work, we study one-dimensional nonlocal elliptic transmission problems with piecewise constant coefficients that may change sign across an interface. In the local setting, we recall the T-coercive structure of the problem and characterize the critical contrast case. In the nonlocal setting, we focus on a simplified configuration in which the cross-interaction coefficient vanishes. Under this assumption, we prove a weak T-coercivity result for the global fractional problem and introduce a reconstructed formulation based on an explicit interface lifting. Then, we consider a simplified finite element discretization of the reconstructed model and prove its convergence toward the classical local transmission problem as the fractional parameter $s\to 1^-$ and the mesh size $h\to 0^+$. Numerical simulations in 1D illustrate the stability and consistency of the method, and a preliminary two-dimensional extension is presented as an exploratory perspective.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 1D nonlocal elliptic transmission problems with sign-changing coefficients, focusing on T-coercivity, reconstructed formulations, and convergence to local problems as fractional parameter approaches 1.
AI.motivation: To study nonlocal elliptic transmission problems with piecewise constant coefficients that may change sign across interfaces, extending local T-coercivity results to the nonlocal setting and developing numerical methods that converge to classical local problems.
AI.method: 1) Recall T-coercive structure in local setting and characterize critical contrast case; 2) For nonlocal setting with vanishing cross-interaction coefficient, prove weak T-coercivity; 3) Introduce reconstructed formulation with explicit interface lifting; 4) Develop simplified finite element discretization; 5) Prove convergence to local transmission problem as s→1⁻ and h→0⁺; 6) Numerical simulations in 1D and preliminary 2D extension.
AI.result: Established weak T-coercivity for nonlocal problems with vanishing cross-interaction, developed reconstructed formulation with interface lifting, proved convergence of discretization to local problem as fractional parameter approaches 1 and mesh refines, demonstrated stability and consistency through 1D numerical simulations, and presented preliminary 2D extension.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully extends T-coercivity concepts from local to nonlocal elliptic transmission problems, develops a reconstructed formulation that enables stable numerical discretization, and proves convergence to classical local problems, providing a bridge between nonlocal and local elliptic transmission theory with sign-changing coefficients.

title: A sign-blocking method for mitigating the fermion sign problem
summary: The fermion sign problem remains the primary obstacle in simulating the thermodynamic properties of various fermionic systems. In this work, we present a sign-blocking method to mitigate the numerical instability inherent in the sign problem. In the sign-blocking method, the Monte Carlo importance sampling remains identical to traditional methods; instead, the sign-blocking method is applied during the post-processing of signed samples. Given the significant progress in simulating the 2D Fermi-Hubbard model over the past decade, a wealth of energy benchmarks is available for comparison. Consequently, we use the 2D Fermi-Hubbard model as a benchmark to validate the sign-blocking method. Surprisingly, our results align exceptionally well with existing state-of-the-art benchmarks, even in regimes previously considered challenging. The physical mechanism of the sign-blocking method lies in uncovering the correlation between energy and sign factors through data blocking, thereby successfully inferring the fermionic system's energy. Our findings suggest that the sign-blocking method holds promise for complex quantum systems, particularly when combined with appropriate simulation techniques such as auxiliary-field formalisms that trace out the fermionic degrees of freedom.

AI.tldr: Sign-blocking method mitigates fermion sign problem in quantum Monte Carlo simulations by post-processing signed samples, validated on 2D Fermi-Hubbard model with excellent agreement to benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The fermion sign problem is the primary obstacle in simulating thermodynamic properties of fermionic systems, causing numerical instability in traditional quantum Monte Carlo methods.
AI.method: Sign-blocking method that applies during post-processing of signed samples while keeping Monte Carlo importance sampling identical to traditional methods. Uses data blocking to uncover correlation between energy and sign factors.
AI.result: Results align exceptionally well with existing state-of-the-art benchmarks for the 2D Fermi-Hubbard model, even in previously challenging regimes.
AI.conclusion: Sign-blocking method shows promise for complex quantum systems, particularly when combined with appropriate simulation techniques like auxiliary-field formalisms that trace out fermionic degrees of freedom.

title: An Adaptive Log-Laguerre Spectral Method for the Radial Dirac Equation: Resolving Asymptotic Decay and Core Singularities in Atomic Calculations
summary: The high-precision solution of the radial Dirac equation is fundamental to relativistic quantum chemistry, essential for reliable pseudopotential generation and all-electron electronic structure methods. However, standard basis-set approaches struggle to simultaneously capture two distinct physical regimes: the non-polynomial singularities at the origin and the state-dependent, multi-scale asymptotic decay of wavefunctions on semi-infinite domains. In this work, we propose a high-precision adaptive spectral-element framework designed to rigorously resolve these spatial challenges. To capture the diverse exponential decay behavior on $[0, \infty)$ without arbitrary domain truncation, an adaptive generalized Laguerre spectral method is introduced, dynamically optimizing the basis scaling factors. Concurrently, near-origin non-polynomial {$r^s$} singularities are resolved utilizing Log-Orthogonal Functions, a basis that intrinsically approximates complex singular behaviors without requiring prior knowledge of the exact analytical exponent {$s$}. Furthermore, the framework incorporates an inverse operator formulation to guarantee spectral purity and eliminate spurious states. Validated across diverse physical regimes, including Coulomb, finite-nucleus, and screened potentials, the proposed method restores exponential convergence and consistently achieves relative accuracies of $10^{-10}$ {in Hartree atomic units or electron volts}. This work provides a robust, non-pollution computational kernel for atomic structure calculations, establishing a numerical standard for generating high-precision atomic data in complex molecular simulations.

AI.tldr: A high-precision adaptive spectral-element framework for solving the radial Dirac equation that handles both near-origin singularities and asymptotic decay without domain truncation, achieving 10^-10 accuracy.
AI.motivation: Standard basis-set approaches fail to simultaneously capture two physical regimes in the radial Dirac equation: non-polynomial singularities at the origin and state-dependent, multi-scale asymptotic decay on semi-infinite domains, which is crucial for reliable pseudopotential generation and all-electron methods in relativistic quantum chemistry.
AI.method: An adaptive spectral-element framework combining: 1) adaptive generalized Laguerre spectral method for [0,∞) domain without truncation, dynamically optimizing basis scaling factors; 2) Log-Orthogonal Functions to handle near-origin r^s singularities without prior knowledge of exact exponent s; 3) inverse operator formulation to ensure spectral purity and eliminate spurious states.
AI.result: The method restores exponential convergence and consistently achieves relative accuracies of 10^-10 in Hartree atomic units or electron volts across diverse physical regimes (Coulomb, finite-nucleus, and screened potentials).
AI.conclusion: This work provides a robust, non-pollution computational kernel for atomic structure calculations, establishing a numerical standard for generating high-precision atomic data in complex molecular simulations.

title: Gyrokinetic equilibria of high temperature superconducting magnetic mirrors
summary: High-temperature superconducting (HTS) magnets and other advances have led to renewed interest in magnetic mirrors for fusion energy. The non-Maxwellian nature of mirror plasmas necessitates kinetic modeling to predict, optimize and design mirrors. Explicit gyrokinetic full-f codes can be used to study instabilities and turbulent transport in tokamaks and mirrors, but they have been prohibitively expensive to integrate directly over the very long time scales required to compute kinetic plasma equilibrium. We demonstrate that these studies are now feasible thanks to novel multiscale methods delivering a 30,000X speed-up. The resulting kinetic equilibrium, electrostatic potential, and ion confinement time are consistent with analytic theory. This transformative capability opens the door to a new way of obtaining equilibria for mirrors, and we discuss how this technique may also accelerate calculations for tokamaks and stellarators. The models presented in this article address critical multiscale problems in modeling magnetic mirrors, opening a new research avenue for equilibrium studies using an explicit continuum gyrokinetic code.

AI.tldr: Novel multiscale methods enable explicit gyrokinetic full-f codes to compute kinetic plasma equilibrium for magnetic mirrors with 30,000X speed-up, making previously prohibitively expensive long-timescale simulations feasible.
AI.motivation: High-temperature superconducting magnets have renewed interest in magnetic mirrors for fusion energy, but kinetic modeling is needed due to non-Maxwellian nature of mirror plasmas. Explicit gyrokinetic codes were too expensive for long timescale equilibrium calculations.
AI.method: Developed novel multiscale methods that accelerate explicit continuum gyrokinetic full-f codes by 30,000X, enabling integration over the very long time scales required for kinetic plasma equilibrium calculations.
AI.result: The method makes kinetic equilibrium studies feasible, with computed kinetic equilibrium, electrostatic potential, and ion confinement time consistent with analytic theory. Achieved 30,000X speed-up over previous approaches.
AI.conclusion: This transformative capability opens new research avenues for equilibrium studies in magnetic mirrors using explicit gyrokinetic codes, and may also accelerate calculations for tokamaks and stellarators, addressing critical multiscale problems in fusion plasma modeling.

title: Inverse Obstacle Scattering from Multi-Frequency Near-Field Backscattering Data
summary: This paper addresses the inverse obstacle scattering problem of simultaneously reconstructing the obstacle geometry and boundary conditions from multi-frequency near-field backscattering data. We first establish rigorous high-frequency asymptotic expansions for the scattered near-field, leveraging pseudo-differential operators (PDOs) to characterize the interaction between wavefront propagation and obstacle boundaries, where the principal symbol of the PDO governs the leading-order behavior of the scattering field. Based on these asymptotic results, we prove a global uniqueness theorem for the simultaneous recovery of the obstacle shape and impedance boundary condition under convexity assumptions. Furthermore, we develop a three-stage numerical reconstruction framework: (1) qualitative shape reconstruction via the direct sampling method; (2) quantitative boundary refinement via shape optimization; and (3) decoupled reconstruction of the boundary condition. A highlight of this algorithm is that all the three steps avoid computing the direct problem. Numerical experiments are presented to verify the robustness and efficiency of the proposed algorithm.

AI.tldr: Simultaneous reconstruction of obstacle geometry and boundary conditions from multi-frequency near-field backscattering data using high-frequency asymptotics and a three-stage numerical framework.
AI.motivation: Addresses the inverse obstacle scattering problem where both obstacle shape and boundary conditions need to be recovered simultaneously from scattering data, which has important applications in non-destructive testing, medical imaging, and remote sensing.
AI.method: 1) Establish high-frequency asymptotic expansions using pseudo-differential operators; 2) Prove global uniqueness theorem for simultaneous recovery; 3) Develop three-stage numerical reconstruction: qualitative shape reconstruction via direct sampling method, quantitative boundary refinement via shape optimization, and decoupled reconstruction of boundary condition.
AI.result: Proves global uniqueness for simultaneous recovery under convexity assumptions. Develops an efficient three-stage algorithm that avoids solving the direct scattering problem. Numerical experiments demonstrate robustness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides both theoretical guarantees and practical algorithms for simultaneous reconstruction of obstacle geometry and boundary conditions from multi-frequency near-field data, with all three reconstruction stages avoiding direct problem computation.

title: A unified sharp-diffusive phase-field model for bulk and interfacial cohesive fracture
summary: In traditional phase-field modeling of multiphase materials, a significant challenge arises from the non-local nature of fracture energy regularization, where interfacial toughness is inherently coupled with the properties of the surrounding bulk phases. Achieving consistency with prescribed material properties typically necessitates complex corrections and exceptionally fine local mesh refinement near the interfaces. To address this fundamental issue, we leverage the capacity of the recently proposed $Ω^2$-model to manifest Dirac-like damage concentration and emergent displacement discontinuities, while introducing an analytical, strongly localized interfacial source term $q_φ$ into the phase-field formulation. It should be emphasized that the ``sharp" nature of the proposed model manifests as a naturally emergent strong discontinuity within a continuum framework, fundamentally distinguishing it from inherently discrete approaches such as cohesive element method. This allows for the independent and precise control of interface toughness in a straightforward manner. Theoretical analysis further reveals that the proposed framework can describe the cohesive failure of both bulk and interfacial regions using a unified set of parametric equations for the cohesive law, where the model parameters are directly determined by the local material properties without the need for additional corrections. The model's versatility is numerically validated through a series of benchmarks. The results confirm that the proposed model not only accurately reproduces diverse interfacial cohesive laws but also captures the intricate competition between interfacial debonding and matrix cracking. This sharp-diffusive phase-field model may provide a robust and computationally efficient tool for predicting complex fracture trajectories in sophisticated engineering materials.

AI.tldr: A sharp-diffusive phase-field model that enables independent control of interface toughness through analytical interfacial source terms, allowing unified description of bulk and interfacial cohesive failure without complex corrections.
AI.motivation: Traditional phase-field models for multiphase materials suffer from non-local fracture energy regularization where interfacial toughness is coupled with bulk properties, requiring complex corrections and fine mesh refinement near interfaces.
AI.method: Leverages the Ω²-model's capacity for Dirac-like damage concentration and emergent displacement discontinuities, while introducing an analytical, strongly localized interfacial source term q_φ into the phase-field formulation to create a sharp-diffusive model.
AI.result: The model enables independent and precise control of interface toughness, can describe cohesive failure of both bulk and interfacial regions using unified parametric equations, and accurately reproduces diverse interfacial cohesive laws while capturing competition between debonding and matrix cracking.
AI.conclusion: The sharp-diffusive phase-field model provides a robust and computationally efficient tool for predicting complex fracture trajectories in sophisticated engineering materials, fundamentally distinguishing itself from discrete approaches like cohesive element methods.

title: A discontinuous Galerkin method with fractal elements
summary: We formulate, analyse, and implement a discontinuous Galerkin finite element method (DG-FEM) for the approximation of the solution of an elliptic boundary value problem in a domain with fractal boundary. We consider the case of the Poisson equation in the Koch snowflake domain with zero Dirichlet boundary conditions, but our methodology can be generalised to other cases. Rather than first approximating the snowflake domain by a polygonal "prefractal'' and then applying a standard DG-FEM on the prefractal, we define a DG-FEM on the snowflake itself, using a geometry-conforming mesh (a fractal tiling) consisting of fractal elements, each similar to the original snowflake. Fluxes across inter-element boundaries, which are fractal curves, are represented in a weak way by integrals over element subdomains. We show how, for local polynomial basis functions, these integrals can be evaluated exactly using the similarity of the elements. We prove well-posedness and quasi-optimality of the method, and provide a partial convergence analysis. We present numerical results for piecewise linear and piecewise quadratic basis functions, which demonstrate the effectiveness of the method.

AI.tldr: A discontinuous Galerkin finite element method is developed for solving Poisson equation on domains with fractal boundaries (Koch snowflake), using fractal-conforming meshes rather than polygonal approximations.
AI.motivation: Standard FEM approaches approximate fractal domains by polygonal prefractals, which may not capture the true fractal geometry accurately. There's a need for methods that directly handle the fractal boundary without approximation.
AI.method: Develop a DG-FEM on the Koch snowflake itself using geometry-conforming fractal tilings. Use fractal elements similar to the original snowflake, represent fluxes across fractal inter-element boundaries weakly via integrals over subdomains, and evaluate integrals exactly using element similarity for polynomial basis functions.
AI.result: Proved well-posedness and quasi-optimality of the method, provided partial convergence analysis. Numerical results for piecewise linear and quadratic basis functions demonstrate method effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The method successfully solves elliptic problems on fractal domains without polygonal approximation, using fractal-conforming meshes and exact integral evaluation via similarity properties, with proven theoretical properties and practical effectiveness.

title: Interaction of Strong Electromagnetic Waves with Unmagnetized Pair Plasmas
summary: We investigate analytically and numerically the interaction of strong electromagnetic waves with unmagnetized pair plasmas. We show that the interaction is governed by a single nonlinearity parameter, $\varepsilon_{\rm p}$, defined as the ratio of the wave strength parameter to the wave frequency in units of the plasma frequency (with both frequencies measured in the plasma rest frame prior to the interaction). When $\varepsilon_{\rm p}<1$, the number of wavelengths that propagate through the plasma without attenuation from induced Compton scattering is approximately $\varepsilon_{\rm p}^{-2/3}$. This attenuation can imprint sub-structures as narrow as a few wavelengths on the pulse profile. When $\varepsilon_{\rm p}>1$, the electromagnetic pulse acts as a relativistic piston and drives a shock into the plasma. Our results establish a framework for the interaction of strong electromagnetic waves with pair plasmas, a process relevant for intense radio pulses from neutron stars and for next-generation pair plasma experiments at multi-petawatt laser facilities.

AI.tldr: Strong electromagnetic waves interacting with unmagnetized pair plasmas exhibit two regimes: weak waves (εₚ<1) experience induced Compton scattering attenuation, while strong waves (εₚ>1) act as relativistic pistons driving shocks.
AI.motivation: Understanding how intense electromagnetic pulses interact with pair plasmas is crucial for astrophysical phenomena like neutron star radio pulses and for laboratory experiments at multi-petawatt laser facilities.
AI.method: Combined analytical and numerical investigation of electromagnetic wave interaction with unmagnetized pair plasmas, focusing on a single nonlinearity parameter εₚ (ratio of wave strength parameter to wave frequency in plasma frequency units).
AI.result: Two distinct regimes: For εₚ<1, waves attenuate via induced Compton scattering with ~εₚ^{-2/3} wavelengths propagating; for εₚ>1, waves act as relativistic pistons driving shocks into plasma. Narrow substructures can form on pulse profiles.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a comprehensive framework for strong electromagnetic wave interactions with pair plasmas, applicable to astrophysical sources and next-generation laser experiments.

title: The Kadomtsev-Petviashvili equation in conformal variables for waves over topography
summary: The conformal mapping approach is a well established technique for solving the Euler equations for potential flows with one spatial dimension. In this work, we extend this framework to problems with a weakly transversal dependence and, by means of asymptotic expansions, obtain a Kadomtsev-Petviashvili type equation formulated in conformal variables as a model for weakly transversal surface waves propagating over topography. A key advantage of this formulation is that the topography, defined in the physical domain, does not need to be a smooth function, or even a function in the classical sense because, our asymptotic analysis relies on the effective depth, which comes through the Jacobian of the conformal map which is assumed to be a slowly varying function. The resulting equation provides a consistent extension of several well known weakly nonlinear dispersive wave models previously reported in the literature. Numerical simulations are performed to illustrate the newly derived equation.

AI.tldr: Extends conformal mapping approach to weakly transversal problems, deriving a KP-type equation in conformal variables for surface waves over topography.
AI.motivation: To extend the well-established conformal mapping technique for 1D potential flows to problems with weak transversal dependence, enabling modeling of surface waves over complex topography.
AI.method: Uses asymptotic expansions to derive a Kadomtsev-Petviashvili (KP) type equation formulated in conformal variables, allowing topography to be non-smooth through the effective depth concept via the Jacobian of the conformal map.
AI.result: Derives a new KP-type equation in conformal variables that consistently extends existing weakly nonlinear dispersive wave models and accommodates non-smooth topography through slowly varying Jacobian.
AI.conclusion: The conformal mapping framework successfully extends to weakly transversal problems, providing a robust model for surface waves over complex topography with numerical validation.

title: Ultrasonic characterization of generally anisotropic elasticity implementing optimal zeroth-order elastic bounds and a wave-fitting approach
summary: The elastic behavior of materials is of critical importance for the design, fabrication, and testing of industrial and structural components. The ease with which the wave angle of incidence can be varied makes ultrasonic techniques well suited for the characterization of anisotropic materials, whose properties are direction-dependent. This work aims to develop an ultrasonic goniometry method in which a wave is transmitted through a sample while scanning over spherical coordinates. A plane-wave model is formulated that accounts for fluid-solid interfaces and is applicable to a wide range of sample thicknesses. The model assumes general anisotropy, enabling the characterization of materials with symmetries up to triclinic, and does not require precise sample alignment. Specially designed transducers support the plane-wave approximation, thereby avoiding the need for more computationally expensive finite-beam models. Furthermore, implementation of the forward model on GPU architectures significantly reduces the computational cost associated with the numerous evaluations required during the waveform fitting inversion. The introduction of optimal zeroth-order bounds is used to tightly delimit the search space, and an isotropic self-consistent solution is shown to provide an effective initial guess. Finally, measurements on plate-like samples are compared with the literature and diffraction-based methods.

AI.tldr: Developed an ultrasonic goniometry method using plane-wave model for anisotropic material characterization with GPU acceleration and efficient inversion techniques.
AI.motivation: Elastic behavior is critical for industrial/structural components, and ultrasonic techniques are well-suited for characterizing anisotropic materials due to variable wave incidence angles.
AI.method: Ultrasonic goniometry with spherical coordinate scanning, plane-wave model accounting for fluid-solid interfaces, specially designed transducers, GPU-accelerated forward model, optimal zeroth-order bounds for search space delimitation, and isotropic self-consistent initial guess.
AI.result: Method enables characterization of materials with symmetries up to triclinic without precise alignment, significantly reduces computational costs, and measurements on plate-like samples are compared with literature and diffraction-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Developed an efficient ultrasonic goniometry method for anisotropic material characterization that combines experimental design, computational optimization, and validation against established techniques.

title: A regularized truncated finite element method for degenerate parabolic stochastic PDE on non-compact graph
summary: We study the numerical approximation of a class of degenerate parabolic stochastic partial differential equations on non-compact metric graphs, which naturally arise in the asymptotic analysis of Hamiltonian flows under small noise perturbations. The numerical discretization of these equations faces several challenges, including the non-compactness of the graph, the degeneracy of the differential operator near vertices, and the non-symmetry of the associated bilinear form. To address these issues, we propose a multi-step numerical strategy combining graph truncation, localized coefficient regularization, and finite element spatial discretization. By incorporating localization techniques, tightness arguments, and resolvent estimates, we establish the strong convergence of the proposed scheme in a weighted $L^2$-space. Our results provide a systematic methodology that is potentially extensible to more general non-compact graphs and degenerate operators.

AI.tldr: Numerical method for degenerate parabolic SPDEs on non-compact metric graphs with convergence proof
AI.motivation: Need to numerically approximate degenerate parabolic SPDEs on non-compact metric graphs that arise from Hamiltonian flow analysis under small noise perturbations
AI.method: Multi-step strategy combining graph truncation, localized coefficient regularization, and finite element spatial discretization with localization techniques, tightness arguments, and resolvent estimates
AI.result: Established strong convergence of the proposed scheme in weighted L²-space
AI.conclusion: Provides systematic methodology extensible to more general non-compact graphs and degenerate operators

title: Ion pickup and velocity space thermalization at outer planet moons
summary: Ion pickup at the outer planets' active moons is a fundamental plasma process in which newly ionized particles from moon exospheres interact with the ambient corotating plasma and are accelerated to match the background flow. Spacecraft observations have revealed intense electromagnetic wave activity commonly attributed to this pickup process. Here we investigate ion pickup using hybrid-kinetic simulations in which ions are treated kinetically while electrons are modeled as a massless fluid. In the moon's rest frame, ambient ions initially stream perpendicular to the background magnetic field at the corotation velocity, creating a nongyrotropic velocity distribution with two ion populations clustered at opposite gyrophases. Within a few ion gyroperiods, this configuration simultaneously excites transverse magnetic perturbations associated with electromagnetic ion cyclotron waves and compressional perturbations associated with mirror-mode and ion Bernstein waves, reaching amplitudes of several percent of the background field strength. Using field-particle correlation analysis, we quantify the energy transfer between waves and particles and demonstrate how these perturbations scatter ions in velocity space, efficiently incorporating newly created ions into the background plasma and leading to isotropization in both gyrophase and pitch angle. These results provide a kinetic framework for understanding pickup-driven wave-particle interactions and offer guidance for interpreting in situ measurements at active moons throughout the outer solar system.

AI.tldr: Hybrid-kinetic simulations reveal how ion pickup at outer planets' moons excites electromagnetic waves (ion cyclotron, mirror-mode, Bernstein waves) that scatter ions in velocity space, efficiently incorporating them into background plasma.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental plasma process of ion pickup at active moons of outer planets, where newly ionized particles from moon exospheres interact with corotating plasma and generate intense electromagnetic wave activity observed by spacecraft.
AI.method: Hybrid-kinetic simulations where ions are treated kinetically while electrons are modeled as a massless fluid, analyzing ion pickup in the moon's rest frame with ambient ions streaming perpendicular to magnetic field at corotation velocity.
AI.result: Within few ion gyroperiods, the nongyrotropic velocity distribution excites transverse magnetic perturbations (EM ion cyclotron waves) and compressional perturbations (mirror-mode and ion Bernstein waves) reaching amplitudes of several percent of background field. Field-particle correlation analysis shows these waves scatter ions in velocity space, efficiently incorporating them into background plasma with isotropization in both gyrophase and pitch angle.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a kinetic framework for understanding pickup-driven wave-particle interactions and offers guidance for interpreting in situ measurements at active moons throughout the outer solar system.

title: Sharp mean Hadamard inequalities and polyconvex integrands that give rise to convex functionals
summary: We investigate several instances of the Hadamard inequality in the mean in two dimensions. As a consequence, we prove the uniqueness of minimizers of an integral functional with a polyconvex integrand, subject to mixed Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions. The theoretical findings are complemented by computational experiments that illustrate the behavior of the minimizers.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates Hadamard inequality instances in 2D, proves uniqueness of minimizers for polyconvex integral functionals with mixed boundary conditions, and validates with computational experiments.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to study Hadamard inequality in the mean in two dimensions and establish uniqueness results for minimizers of integral functionals with polyconvex integrands under mixed Dirichlet-Neumann boundary conditions.
AI.method: The method involves theoretical analysis of Hadamard inequality instances in 2D, proving uniqueness of minimizers for polyconvex integral functionals, and complementing with computational experiments to illustrate minimizer behavior.
AI.result: The main result is proving the uniqueness of minimizers for the specified integral functional with polyconvex integrand subject to mixed boundary conditions, supported by computational validation.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes theoretical uniqueness results for minimizers in polyconvex variational problems with mixed boundary conditions, with computational experiments confirming the theoretical findings.

title: How Does Intercalation Reshape Layered Structures? A First-Principles Study of Sodium Insertion in Layered Potassium Birnessite
summary: This study presents a first-principles study at the level of hybrid-level density functional theory of the sodium intercalation process in a layered potassium birnessite (a layered manganese dioxide, δ-MnO2). Understanding the intercalation processes of δ-MnO2 is a vital step in advancing its potential innovative applications. Through a formation energy formalism, we analyze the stability of the structure as sodium ions (Na+) are intercalated between layers. Simulated Raman spectra allow us to find relationships between the vibrational and structural properties of the material, i.e. we identify the most important vibrational modes and related them to the structural/geometrical change. The diffusion of Na+ and K+ ions in birnessite is studied by transition state theory, determining the energy barriers to ion displacement in the interlayer. The symmetry and planar density of the system are characterized by simulated X-ray diffraction and geometrical analysis of the optimized structures. Through binding energy analysis, we also find that the Na+ ions are more loosely bound to the lattice as they reach the saturation limit. Finally, the electronic properties are studied via spin-polarized densities of states. As intercalants are added, the electronic properties are profoundly modified, resulting from modification of Mn oxidation states, lattice distortions, and symmetry effects. Moreover, some of the intercalated structures behave as bipolar magnetic semiconductors with potential applications in spintronics devices. In other words, the band gaps and magnetic behavior of the system can be controlled by intercalation. This work provides an overarching analysis of intercalated birnessite and describes the essential properties of potassium birnessite and co-intercalation with Sodium as a next-generation energy, electronic, and spintronic material.

AI.tldr: First-principles DFT study of sodium intercalation in potassium birnessite (δ-MnO2), analyzing structural stability, vibrational properties, ion diffusion, electronic structure, and magnetic behavior.
AI.motivation: To understand sodium intercalation processes in δ-MnO2 for advancing its potential applications in energy storage, electronics, and spintronics.
AI.method: Hybrid-level density functional theory calculations with formation energy formalism, simulated Raman spectra, transition state theory for ion diffusion, simulated XRD, binding energy analysis, and spin-polarized density of states.
AI.result: Na+ ions become more loosely bound at saturation limit; intercalation modifies electronic properties via Mn oxidation state changes and lattice distortions; some structures behave as bipolar magnetic semiconductors; band gaps and magnetic behavior can be controlled by intercalation.
AI.conclusion: Provides comprehensive analysis of intercalated birnessite, demonstrating potassium birnessite with sodium co-intercalation as promising material for next-generation energy, electronic, and spintronic applications.

title: From Exact Space-Time Symmetry Conservation to Automatic Mesh Refinement in Discrete Initial Boundary Value Problems
comment: 18 pages, 8 figures, Talk given at minisymposium MS127 at the 15th International Conference on Spectral and High Order Methods (ICOSAHOM 2025), July 17th 2025, McGill University, Montreal, Canada
summary: In this contribution we present recent developments in the formulation and solution of Initial Boundary Value Problems (IBVPs). Building upon a modern variational action formulation of classical dynamics, we treat Initial Boundary Value Problems directly on the action level, bypassing governing equations. We show that by including coordinate maps as dynamical degrees of freedom together with propagating fields two key results emerge. Space-time symmetries remain protected even after discretization, leading to an exact conservation of Noether charges even for discrete IBVPs. The dynamical nature of the coordinate maps leads to an adjustment of space-time resolution, guided by Noether charge conservation, realizing a form of automatic adaptive mesh refinement. We stress that as long as SBP operators are used for the discretization, our results are independent of whether the dynamics are solved on the action or governing equation level and hold in particular also at high order. As proof-of-principle for our approach we present its application to scalar wave-propagation in 1+1 dimensions.

AI.tldr: A novel variational approach to Initial Boundary Value Problems that preserves space-time symmetries after discretization and enables automatic adaptive mesh refinement through dynamical coordinate maps.
AI.motivation: To develop a more fundamental approach to solving Initial Boundary Value Problems that preserves important physical properties like space-time symmetries even after numerical discretization, and to enable automatic adaptive mesh refinement without compromising conservation laws.
