AI.method: Uses a modern variational action formulation of classical dynamics, treating IBVPs directly at the action level rather than through governing equations. Includes coordinate maps as dynamical degrees of freedom alongside propagating fields, and employs Summation-By-Parts (SBP) operators for discretization.
AI.result: Two key results emerge: 1) Space-time symmetries remain protected after discretization, leading to exact conservation of Noether charges even for discrete IBVPs; 2) The dynamical nature of coordinate maps enables automatic adaptive mesh refinement guided by Noether charge conservation. The approach works with SBP operators regardless of whether dynamics are solved at action or governing equation level, including high-order discretizations.
AI.conclusion: The variational action-level approach to IBVPs provides a fundamental framework that preserves important physical symmetries and conservation laws after discretization while enabling automatic mesh adaptation, with successful proof-of-principle demonstration for scalar wave propagation in 1+1 dimensions.

title: A particular solution of a higher-order non-homogeneous Cauchy-Euler equation
summary: In this paper we introduce a new concept of atoms on discrete sets to develop an advanced method to find a particular solution for higher-order non-homogeneous Cauchy-Euler equations. The proposed method provides also an approximate solution by using approximate roots for the characteristic polynomial of the Cauchy-Euler equation.

AI.tldr: New method using discrete atoms to find particular solutions for higher-order non-homogeneous Cauchy-Euler equations, with approximate solutions via approximate characteristic roots.
AI.motivation: To develop a more advanced approach for solving higher-order non-homogeneous Cauchy-Euler equations, which are important in mathematical physics and engineering applications.
AI.method: Introduces a new concept of atoms on discrete sets to develop a method for finding particular solutions. Uses approximate roots of the characteristic polynomial when exact roots are unavailable.
AI.result: Proposes a novel method that can handle higher-order non-homogeneous Cauchy-Euler equations and provides both exact and approximate solutions.
AI.conclusion: The new atom-based approach offers an advanced solution technique for Cauchy-Euler equations, with practical applicability through approximate methods when exact solutions are difficult to obtain.

title: Unified Gas-Kinetic Scheme for Unsteady Multiscale Flows with Moving Boundaries
summary: Simulating multiscale flows with moving boundaries, such as hypersonic multi-body separation and flows in micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS), requires robust numerical methods that couple mesh deformation with complex flow physics. This paper presents a hybrid overlapping moving-mesh technique developed within the unified gas-kinetic scheme (UGKS). To mitigate the Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy (CFL) constraint, we extend the implicit unsteady UGKS solver to support moving meshes, incorporating memory-efficient data handling and parallel computing optimizations to maximize computational efficiency. Validated against hypersonic multi-body separation and thermal rarefied MEMS flows, the proposed scheme accurately resolves complex, dynamic multiscale phenomena. The results confirm that this robust and efficient method provides a highly reliable tool for modeling dynamic flow interactions in complex geometric configurations.

AI.tldr: Hybrid overlapping moving-mesh technique within UGKS for simulating multiscale flows with moving boundaries, addressing CFL constraints through implicit solver and computational optimizations.
AI.motivation: Need robust numerical methods for simulating multiscale flows with moving boundaries in applications like hypersonic multi-body separation and MEMS flows, which require coupling mesh deformation with complex flow physics.
AI.method: Developed hybrid overlapping moving-mesh technique within unified gas-kinetic scheme (UGKS), extended implicit unsteady UGKS solver to support moving meshes, incorporated memory-efficient data handling and parallel computing optimizations.
AI.result: Validated against hypersonic multi-body separation and thermal rarefied MEMS flows, accurately resolves complex dynamic multiscale phenomena, confirms robust and efficient performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scheme provides a highly reliable tool for modeling dynamic flow interactions in complex geometric configurations, offering robust and efficient simulation capabilities.

title: Numerical analysis for the Stokes problem with non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary condition
summary:
The Stokes problem with non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary condition is solved numerically using conforming discretizations and an approximation of the boundary datum in the corresponding trace space. Optimal discretization error estimates are derived. The theory accounts for the influence of corner singularities in the case of a non-convex domain. Several variants of the boundary data approximation are discussed. Moreover, the case of boundary data with very low regularity is studied, where a weak solution does not exist. The well-posedness of the very weak solution is investigated, and optimal discretization error estimates are derived. Numerical tests confirm the theory.
  The compatibility condition for the boundary data is not necessary for well-posedness of the weak and very weak formulations but it ensures that the solution satisfies the continuity equation in the distributional sense. In the same spirit, the compatibility condition is not necessary for the approximating boundary data; a good approximation of the original boundary data is important.

AI.tldr: Numerical solution of Stokes problem with non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions using conforming discretizations, with analysis of boundary data approximation, corner singularities, and low regularity cases.
AI.motivation: To develop robust numerical methods for solving Stokes problems with non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions, particularly addressing challenges like boundary data approximation, domain corner singularities, and cases where boundary data has very low regularity.
AI.method: Uses conforming discretizations with approximation of boundary datum in corresponding trace space. Studies several variants of boundary data approximation. Investigates both weak and very weak solution formulations for low regularity cases.
AI.result: Derives optimal discretization error estimates accounting for corner singularities in non-convex domains. Shows compatibility condition is not necessary for well-posedness but ensures continuity equation satisfaction. Confirms theory with numerical tests.
AI.conclusion: The method provides robust numerical solution for Stokes problems with non-homogeneous boundary conditions, handling various regularity levels and domain complexities. Good approximation of boundary data is more important than satisfying compatibility conditions.

title: Parabolic--Elliptic Dynamics with Local--Nonlocal Coupled Operators
summary: In this paper, we study two local--nonlocal settings for parabolic--elliptic evolution systems. In our problems we have a disjoint partition of the spacial domain $Ω$ as $Ω=A\cup B$ and we first consider a local parabolic equation posed in $A$ with a nonlocal elliptic balance equation acting in the complementary subdomain $B$. Next, we reverse the roles and take a local elliptic equation posed in $A$ coupled with a nonlocal parabolic equation acting in $B$. In both models, the interaction between the two regions is driven by a nonlocal transmission term given by a kernel that transfers mass across the interface, giving rise to a mixed local--nonlocal, elliptic--parabolic dynamics. We consider Neumann boundary conditions for both problems. To being our analysis we first establish the existence and uniqueness of solutions using a fixed point argument. Then, we provide a detailed analysis of their qualitative behavior. In particular, we show that the coupling structure induces a natural energy functional whose gradient flow governs the evolution, despite the elliptic--parabolic nature of the system. As it is expected in Neumann settings, we prove that the total mass in the whole domain $Ω$ is preserved in time. We also analyze the long-time behaviour and obtain decay estimates for the parabolic component, which in turn drive the convergence of the elliptic part to a constant solution. Finally, we prove that the parabolic--elliptic problem under consideration is the limit of a purely parabolic problem when a parameter that controls the speed of the dynamic at which one component evolves goes to zero.

AI.tldr: Analysis of two mixed local-nonlocal parabolic-elliptic systems with Neumann conditions, showing existence, uniqueness, energy structure, mass conservation, long-time behavior, and parabolic limit.
AI.motivation: To study coupled local-nonlocal evolution systems where spatial domain is partitioned, with different dynamics (parabolic vs elliptic) in each subdomain, interacting through nonlocal transmission terms.
AI.method: Two models: 1) local parabolic in A with nonlocal elliptic in B, 2) local elliptic in A with nonlocal parabolic in B. Both use Neumann BCs and kernel-based nonlocal transmission. Analysis via fixed point arguments for existence/uniqueness, energy functional approach, and asymptotic analysis.
AI.result: Existence/uniqueness established via fixed point; systems behave as gradient flows of natural energy functional; total mass conserved; parabolic component decays driving elliptic part to constant; parabolic-elliptic system is limit of purely parabolic problem as evolution speed parameter vanishes.
AI.conclusion: Mixed local-nonlocal parabolic-elliptic systems with Neumann conditions exhibit well-posedness, gradient flow structure, mass conservation, and convergence to equilibrium, with parabolic-elliptic model emerging as singular limit of purely parabolic dynamics.

title: Accelerated Dopant Screening in Oxide Semiconductors via Multi-Fidelity Contextual Bandits and a Three-Tier DFT Validation Funnel
summary:
Band gap engineering of oxide semiconductors through doping is critical for photocatalysis and optoelectronics, yet the combinatorial space of dopant elements, substitution sites, and co-doping combinations far exceeds typical density functional theory (DFT) budgets. We screen doped candidates across five oxide hosts (ZnO, TiO2, SrTiO3, SnO2, MgO), culminating in a 529-candidate ZnO co-doping campaign, and identify Cu-containing co-doped ZnO systems as consistently achieving visible-light-range band gaps (1.0-1.8 eV), with Y2Cu2 co-doped ZnO as the optimal candidate (1.84 eV). A three-tier validation funnel (PBE, PBE+U, ionic relaxation) reveals that no single level of theory suffices: V-doped ZnO shifts from near-metallic to wide-gap upon Hubbard U correction, while Cu-doped SrTiO3 enters the visible-light window only after correcting for d-electron localization.
  To make this screening tractable, we introduce a multi-fidelity screening strategy that replaces 81% of DFT evaluations with computationally inexpensive surrogate predictions, reducing a 529-candidate closed-loop Quantum ESPRESSO campaign from an estimated 440 to 62 CPU-hours while finding the global optimum in 100% of 50 independent trials (p = 5.0e-8 versus random screening, Wilcoxon signed-rank). Cross-host analysis of the dopant-host interaction matrix reveals that dopant performance is governed by just two latent chemical dimensions, enabling prediction of rankings in unseen hosts. All 583 DFT calculations, screening code, and stability proofs are released as an open benchmark.

AI.tldr: Multi-fidelity screening strategy accelerates band gap engineering of doped oxide semiconductors, identifying optimal visible-light photocatalysts while reducing computational cost by 86%.
AI.motivation: Band gap engineering through doping is crucial for photocatalysis and optoelectronics, but the combinatorial space of dopants, sites, and co-doping combinations is too large for conventional DFT screening.
AI.method: Developed a multi-fidelity screening strategy that replaces 81% of expensive DFT calculations with inexpensive surrogate predictions. Screened doped candidates across five oxide hosts (ZnO, TiO2, SrTiO3, SnO2, MgO) with a three-tier validation funnel (PBE, PBE+U, ionic relaxation).
AI.result: Identified Cu-containing co-doped ZnO systems achieving visible-light band gaps (1.0-1.8 eV), with Y2Cu2 co-doped ZnO as optimal (1.84 eV). Reduced computational cost from 440 to 62 CPU-hours while maintaining 100% success rate in finding global optimum. Discovered dopant performance governed by just two latent chemical dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Multi-fidelity screening enables efficient exploration of large dopant spaces, revealing that no single level of theory suffices for accurate band gap prediction. The approach provides a practical framework for materials discovery with released open benchmark data.

title: DiLO: Decoupling Generative Priors and Neural Operators via Diffusion Latent Optimization for Inverse Problems
summary: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful generative priors for solving PDE-constrained inverse problems. Compared to end-to-end approaches relying on massive paired datasets, explicitly decoupling the prior distribution of physical parameters from the forward physical model, a paradigm often formalized as Plug-and-Play (PnP) priors, offers enhanced flexibility and generalization. To accelerate inference within such decoupled frameworks, fast neural operators are employed as surrogate solvers. However, directly integrating them into standard diffusion sampling introduces a critical bottleneck: evaluating neural surrogates on partially denoised, non-physical intermediate states forces them into out-of-distribution (OOD) regimes. To eliminate this, the physical surrogate must be evaluated exclusively on the fully denoised parameter, a principle we formalize as the Manifold Consistency Requirement. To satisfy this requirement, we present Diffusion Latent Optimization (DiLO), which transforms the stochastic sampling process into a deterministic latent trajectory, enabling stable backpropagation of measurement gradients to the initial latent state. By keeping the trajectory on the physical manifold, it ensures physically valid updates and improves reconstruction accuracy. We provide theoretical guarantees for the convergence of this optimization trajectory. Extensive experiments across Electrical Impedance Tomography, Inverse Scattering, and Inverse Navier-Stokes problems demonstrate DiLO's accuracy, efficiency, and robustness to noise.

AI.tldr: DiLO addresses the OOD problem in diffusion-based PDE inverse problems by enforcing manifold consistency, transforming stochastic sampling into deterministic latent optimization for stable gradient backpropagation.
AI.motivation: Standard diffusion sampling with neural surrogate solvers fails because evaluating surrogates on partially denoised intermediate states pushes them into out-of-distribution regimes, violating the Manifold Consistency Requirement for physically valid updates.
AI.method: DiLO transforms stochastic diffusion sampling into a deterministic latent trajectory optimization, enabling stable backpropagation of measurement gradients to the initial latent state while keeping updates on the physical manifold.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Electrical Impedance Tomography, Inverse Scattering, and Inverse Navier-Stokes problems demonstrate DiLO's superior accuracy, efficiency, and robustness to noise compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: DiLO successfully addresses the OOD bottleneck in diffusion-based PDE inverse problems by enforcing manifold consistency through latent optimization, providing theoretical convergence guarantees and practical improvements across diverse physical inverse problems.

title: Stochastic homogenization of fractional obstacle problems
summary: We prove a stochastic homogenization result for a class of \emph{nonlinear} and \emph{nonlocal} variational problems in domains with many small randomly distributed (bilateral) obstacles. Our model case is a Dirichlet problem for the \emph{fractional} $p$-Laplacian, $p>1$, where a pinning condition $u=0$ is imposed on the solution in a \emph{random} collection of small balls whose centers and radii are generated by a \emph{stationary marked point process}. Such a general obstacle distribution allows for \emph{clustering effects} to appear with positive probability. Under suitable moment conditions on the obstacle radii, we identify a critical scaling regime in which the fractional $p$-capacity density of the obstacles is asymptotically additive \emph{almost surely}. In turn, this key property allows us to derive an effective homogenized problem which is formally analogous to the one obtained in the periodic setting or under the assumption of well-separation for the obstacles. The analysis also extends to the case of \emph{randomly shaped obstacles} and to a broad class of \emph{nonlocal interaction kernels}. At the methodological level, the paper develops a streamlined proof strategy with several new ingredients, among them the use of Palm measures.

AI.tldr: The paper proves stochastic homogenization for nonlinear nonlocal variational problems with randomly distributed obstacles, focusing on fractional p-Laplacian with pinning conditions on random small balls, allowing clustering effects.
AI.motivation: To extend homogenization theory to nonlinear nonlocal problems with random obstacles that can cluster, going beyond periodic or well-separated obstacle distributions typically studied in existing literature.
AI.method: Uses stochastic analysis with stationary marked point processes to model obstacle distribution, identifies critical scaling regime for obstacle radii, employs Palm measures and establishes asymptotic additivity of fractional p-capacity density almost surely.
AI.result: Derives effective homogenized problem analogous to periodic/well-separated cases, extends to randomly shaped obstacles and broad class of nonlocal kernels, develops streamlined proof strategy with new tools including Palm measures.
AI.conclusion: Successfully establishes stochastic homogenization for nonlinear nonlocal variational problems with clustering random obstacles, providing general framework that includes fractional p-Laplacian as model case and extends to various obstacle shapes and interaction kernels.

title: Continuous PT-Symmetry Breaking as a Design Variable for Giant Altermagnetic Spin Splitting
summary: Magnetic point-group analysis classifies altermagnets but returns only a binary symmetry verdict, leaving spin-splitting energy (SSE) inaccessible without spin-polarized density functional theory (DFT). This binary ceiling is not fundamental. Sublattice symmetry breaking is promoted here to a continuous, DFT-free scalar -- the Motif Symmetry-Breaking Index (MSBI) -- that quantifies $\mathcal{PT}$-symmetry breaking between antiparallel magnetic motifs directly from crystal coordinates. SHAP analysis of an XGBoost surrogate trained on 3,851 DFT-labeled binary structures identifies three dominant descriptors: MSBI (symmetry-breaking axis), motif packing fraction MPF (superexchange axis), and the $p/d$ electron ratio (covalency axis), each mapping onto a directly tunable experimental handle. A controlled VO--CrSb comparison within the same P$6_3$/mmc host lattice demonstrates that composition alone boosts SSE sevenfold. Bayesian optimization over this three-axis space, followed by independent DFT validation, recovers $α$-NiS (SSE $= 0.823$\,eV) as cross-validation against an independent symmetry-based prediction and identifies three previously unrecognized high-SSE candidates -- square-planar FeS (1.297\,eV), octahedral CoS (1.103\,eV), and FeAs (1.089\,eV) -- all matching or exceeding CrSb. Square-planar Fe--S is proposed as a transferable coordination motif for giant altermagnetic spin splitting, advancing altermagnet design from symmetry classification to continuous quantitative optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a continuous Motif Symmetry-Breaking Index (MSBI) to quantify altermagnetic spin-splitting energy without DFT, identifies three key experimental handles, and discovers new high-SSE materials.
AI.motivation: Current magnetic point-group analysis only provides binary symmetry classification for altermagnets, leaving spin-splitting energy inaccessible without computationally expensive spin-polarized DFT calculations. There's a need for a continuous, DFT-free metric to quantify spin-splitting and enable systematic material design.
AI.method: Developed MSBI as a continuous scalar quantifying PT-symmetry breaking between antiparallel magnetic motifs from crystal coordinates alone. Used SHAP analysis on XGBoost surrogate trained on 3,851 DFT-labeled binary structures to identify dominant descriptors. Conducted controlled VO-CrSb comparison and Bayesian optimization over the three-axis design space.
AI.result: Identified three dominant experimental handles: MSBI (symmetry-breaking), motif packing fraction (superexchange), and p/d electron ratio (covalency). Composition variation in same host lattice boosted SSE sevenfold. Bayesian optimization recovered α-NiS (SSE = 0.823 eV) and discovered three new high-SSE candidates: square-planar FeS (1.297 eV), octahedral CoS (1.103 eV), and FeAs (1.089 eV), all matching/exceeding CrSb.
AI.conclusion: The MSBI framework advances altermagnet design from binary symmetry classification to continuous quantitative optimization. Square-planar Fe-S coordination is proposed as a transferable motif for giant altermagnetic spin splitting, enabling systematic material discovery and experimental tuning.

title: Data-efficient extraction of optical properties from 3D Monte Carlo TPSFs using Bi-LSTM transfer learning
summary: Time-Resolved Spectroscopy (TRS) is a powerful modality for non-invasive characterization of turbid media. However, extracting optical properties, absorption $μ_a$ and reduced scattering $μ_s'$, from 3D stochastic measurements remains computationally expensive for real-time applications. In this paper, we propose a data-efficient, physics-informed transfer learning strategy using a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) network. By leveraging a fast deterministic solver to establish a physical prior before fine-tuning on a restricted set of 3D Monte Carlo simulations, our model successfully bridges the analytical-to-stochastic domain gap. The proposed method eliminates the systematic bias of analytical models while maintaining a competitive error with near-instantaneous inference time.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed Bi-LSTM network with transfer learning enables fast, accurate optical property extraction from 3D stochastic TRS measurements by bridging analytical-to-stochastic domain gap.
AI.motivation: Current methods for extracting optical properties (absorption μ_a and reduced scattering μ_s') from 3D stochastic Time-Resolved Spectroscopy measurements are computationally expensive, limiting real-time applications.
AI.method: Data-efficient, physics-informed transfer learning using Bidirectional LSTM network. First leverages fast deterministic solver to establish physical prior, then fine-tunes on limited set of 3D Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.result: Eliminates systematic bias of analytical models while maintaining competitive error with near-instantaneous inference time. Successfully bridges analytical-to-stochastic domain gap.
AI.conclusion: The proposed transfer learning approach enables fast, accurate optical property extraction from 3D stochastic TRS measurements, making real-time applications feasible.

title: On Kirchhoff-type p(.)-Laplacian problems withsandwich-type and arbitrary growth
summary: We establish the existence of a positive bounded weak solution for a class of Kirchhoff-type $p(\cdot)$-Laplacian problems involving an arbitrary growth and a sandwich-type growth $s(\cdot)\in (\inf p,\sup p)$. This setting leads to substantial analytical difficulties in the variational analysis of the associated energy functional. By combining truncation arguments with a priori estimates, we prove the existence result under suitable assumptions on the data.

AI.tldr: Existence of positive bounded weak solution for Kirchhoff-type p(·)-Laplacian problems with arbitrary and sandwich-type growth conditions.
AI.motivation: Address analytical difficulties in variational analysis of energy functionals for Kirchhoff-type p(·)-Laplacian problems with complex growth conditions, particularly when dealing with sandwich-type growth s(·) ∈ (inf p, sup p).
AI.method: Combination of truncation arguments with a priori estimates to handle the substantial analytical difficulties in the variational analysis of the associated energy functional.
AI.result: Established existence of a positive bounded weak solution under suitable assumptions on the data for this class of problems.
AI.conclusion: Successfully proved existence result for Kirchhoff-type p(·)-Laplacian problems with arbitrary and sandwich-type growth conditions using truncation and a priori estimate techniques.

title: Scalable Generative Sampling and Multilevel Estimation for Lattice Field Theories Near Criticality
comment: 15 pages, 8 figures, 8 tables. Code to be released soon
summary: Sampling lattice field theories near criticality is severely hindered by critical slowing down, which makes standard Markov chain methods increasingly inefficient at large lattice volumes. We introduce a multiscale generative sampler, inspired by renormalization-group ideas, that models the Boltzmann distribution through a coarse-to-fine hierarchy across length scales. At each level, a conditional Gaussian mixture model captures the main local dependence of newly introduced variables on the already-sampled coarse field, while a masked continuous normalizing flow refines the remaining conditional structure. Coarse levels encode the dominant long-wavelength modes, and finer levels progressively add short-distance fluctuations. In addition, because the architecture preserves coarse fields exactly during refinement, it provides exact restriction maps at no additional computational cost and directly enables unbiased Multilevel Monte Carlo (MLMC) variance reduction. For the two-dimensional scalar $φ^4$ theory at criticality, the method achieves integrated autocorrelation times orders of magnitude smaller than Hybrid Monte Carlo (HMC) on large volumes, maintains high importance-sampling efficiency relative to other generative baselines, and reproduces unbiased physical observables in statistical agreement with long HMC simulations.

AI.tldr: A multiscale generative sampler using renormalization-group ideas with conditional Gaussian mixtures and masked normalizing flows achieves dramatically reduced autocorrelation times for critical lattice field theories compared to HMC.
AI.motivation: Standard Markov chain methods like HMC suffer from critical slowing down near criticality in lattice field theories, becoming inefficient at large volumes. There's a need for more efficient sampling methods that can handle critical systems.
AI.method: Multiscale generative sampler inspired by renormalization-group: coarse-to-fine hierarchy with conditional Gaussian mixture models capturing local dependence, masked continuous normalizing flows refining conditional structure. Coarse levels encode long-wavelength modes, finer levels add short-distance fluctuations. Architecture preserves coarse fields exactly during refinement.
AI.result: For 2D scalar φ⁴ theory at criticality: orders of magnitude smaller integrated autocorrelation times than HMC on large volumes; high importance-sampling efficiency relative to other generative baselines; reproduces unbiased physical observables in statistical agreement with long HMC simulations.
AI.conclusion: The multiscale generative sampler effectively addresses critical slowing down, enables exact restriction maps for unbiased MLMC variance reduction, and provides efficient sampling for critical lattice field theories while maintaining statistical accuracy.

title: A tensor-based exponential integrator for diffusion--reaction equations in common curvilinear coordinates
summary: In this paper, we study a tensor-based method for the numerical solution of a class of diffusion--reaction equations defined on spatial domains that admit common curvilinear coordinate representations. Typical examples in 2D include disks (polar coordinates), and in 3D balls or cylinders (spherical or cylindrical coordinates) as well as spheres for problems involving the Laplace--Beltrami operator. The proposed approach is based on a carefully chosen finite difference discretization of the Laplace operators that yields matrices with a structured representation as sums of Kronecker products. For the time integration, we introduce a novel split variant of the exponential Euler method that effectively handles the stiffness and avoids the severe time step size restriction of classical explicit methods. By exploiting the peculiar form of the obtained discretized operators and the chosen splitting strategy, we compute the needed action of the $\varphi_1$ matrix function through suitable tensor-matrix products in a $μ$-mode framework. We demonstrate the efficiency the approach on a wide range of physically relevant 2D and 3D examples of coupled diffusion--reaction systems generating Turing patterns with up to $10^6$ degrees of freedom.

AI.tldr: Tensor-based method for solving diffusion-reaction equations on curvilinear domains using structured finite differences and split exponential Euler time integration.
AI.motivation: To efficiently solve diffusion-reaction equations on complex curvilinear domains (disks, balls, cylinders, spheres) that are common in physical applications, avoiding severe time step restrictions of classical methods.
AI.method: Finite difference discretization yielding matrices as sums of Kronecker products, combined with novel split exponential Euler method for time integration, using μ-mode tensor-matrix products to compute φ₁ matrix function actions.
AI.result: Demonstrated efficiency on physically relevant 2D/3D examples of coupled diffusion-reaction systems generating Turing patterns with up to 10⁶ degrees of freedom.
AI.conclusion: The tensor-based approach with split exponential Euler method provides an efficient numerical solution for diffusion-reaction equations on curvilinear domains, handling stiffness effectively while avoiding severe time step restrictions.

title: Time-dimensional reduction and a Carleman contraction principle for an inverse initial data problem for the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations with unknown body force
summary: We solve an inverse initial data problem for the incompressible Navier-Stokes system. The objective is to recover the initial velocity and pressure from lateral boundary observations, without assuming that the time-independent body force is known. To eliminate this unknown force, we differentiate the momentum equation with respect to time and then apply a Legendre polynomial-exponential time-dimensional reduction. This procedure yields a coupled system of elliptic equations for the expansion coefficients. We then construct a contractive map for this reduced system on a suitable admissible set equipped with a Carleman-weighted norm. Its fixed point yields an approximate solution of the time-dimensional reduction model, and the contraction property gives rise to a globally convergent Picard iteration. Finally, we present a numerical algorithm based on this framework and numerical experiments showing accurate reconstructions of the initial velocity and pressure from synthetic boundary data.

AI.tldr: Recover initial velocity and pressure for Navier-Stokes from boundary data without knowing body force, using time differentiation and Legendre polynomial-exponential reduction.
AI.motivation: Solve inverse initial data problem for incompressible Navier-Stokes system using only lateral boundary observations, without requiring knowledge of the time-independent body force.
AI.method: Differentiate momentum equation with time, apply Legendre polynomial-exponential time-dimensional reduction to get elliptic system, construct contractive map with Carleman-weighted norm, use globally convergent Picard iteration.
AI.result: Developed numerical algorithm with accurate reconstructions of initial velocity and pressure from synthetic boundary data using the proposed framework.
AI.conclusion: Successfully solved inverse initial data problem for Navier-Stokes without known body force using time-dimensional reduction and contractive mapping approach with numerical validation.

title: Dynamic multiphase flow triggers chaotic mixing in porous media
summary: Solute mixing plays a pivotal role in a broad spectrum of chemical and biological processes across natural and engineered porous media. However, current understanding of mixing dynamics remains largely constrained to steady flows in fully or partially water-saturated environments. Multiphase flow systems are generally unsteady, with moving fluid interfaces and flow paths that change in time. Despite the widespread occurrence of dynamic multiphase flows, their impacts on solute mixing are largely unknown. Here, we use experiments and numerical simulations to investigate the effect of dynamic two-phase flow on the stretching and folding of fluid elements, a fundamental mechanism driving solute mixing and reactions in porous media. We find that dynamic two-phase flows induce chaotic mixing, characterized by exponential stretching of fluid elements, leading to strongly enhanced mixing compared to steady single phase flows. By extensive numerical multiphase flow simulations, we establish dynamic steady states where we reliably measure the mean fluid stretching rate as a function of flow rate. We show that stretching is maximized at an optimum flow rate which balances fluid shear deformation against the frequency of flow reorientation by the intermittent motion of the fluid interface. The findings are rationalized by a mechanistic model linking basic multiphase flow characteristics to the stretching rate, opening new perspectives to understand and control mixing and reactions in a wide range of multiphase flow systems.

AI.tldr: Dynamic two-phase flows in porous media induce chaotic mixing with exponential stretching of fluid elements, enhancing mixing beyond steady single-phase flows, with optimal flow rates balancing shear deformation and flow reorientation frequency.
AI.motivation: Current understanding of solute mixing is limited to steady flows in water-saturated environments, while multiphase flow systems are dynamic with moving interfaces and changing flow paths. The impact of these dynamic multiphase flows on solute mixing remains largely unknown despite their widespread occurrence.
AI.method: Combined experimental and numerical simulation approach to investigate dynamic two-phase flow effects on fluid element stretching and folding. Extensive numerical multiphase flow simulations establish dynamic steady states to measure mean fluid stretching rates as a function of flow rate.
AI.result: Dynamic two-phase flows induce chaotic mixing characterized by exponential stretching of fluid elements, leading to strongly enhanced mixing compared to steady single-phase flows. Stretching is maximized at an optimum flow rate that balances fluid shear deformation against the frequency of flow reorientation by intermittent motion of fluid interfaces.
AI.conclusion: The findings are rationalized by a mechanistic model linking basic multiphase flow characteristics to stretching rates, opening new perspectives to understand and control mixing and reactions in a wide range of multiphase flow systems.

title: Asymptotic-Preserving and Well-Balanced Linearly Implicit IMEX Schemes for the Anelastic Limit of the Isentropic Euler Equations with Gravity
summary: We consider the compressible Euler system with anelastic scaling, modeling isentropic flows under the influence of gravity. In the zero-Mach-number limit, the solution of the compressible Euler system converges to a variable density anelastic incompressible limit system. In this work, we present the design and analysis of a class of higher-order linearly implicit IMEX Runge-Kutta schemes that are asymptotic preserving, i.e., they respect the transitory nature of the governing equations in the limit. The presence of gravitational potential warrants the incorporation of the well-balancing property. The scheme is developed as a novel combination of a penalization of a linear steady state, a finite-volume balance-preserving reconstruction, and a source term discretization preserving steady states. The penalization plays a crucial role in obtaining a linearly implicit scheme, and well-balanced flux-source discretization ensures accuracy in very low Mach number regimes. Some results of numerical case studies are presented to corroborate the theoretical assertions.

AI.tldr: Higher-order linearly implicit IMEX Runge-Kutta schemes for compressible Euler with anelastic scaling, designed to be asymptotic preserving and well-balanced for gravitational flows in zero-Mach-number limit.
AI.motivation: To develop numerical schemes that can accurately handle compressible Euler equations with gravity in the zero-Mach-number limit, where solutions converge to variable density anelastic incompressible systems, while maintaining asymptotic preserving and well-balanced properties.
AI.method: Novel combination of penalization of linear steady state, finite-volume balance-preserving reconstruction, and source term discretization preserving steady states. Uses linearly implicit IMEX Runge-Kutta schemes with penalization enabling linear implicitness and well-balanced flux-source discretization for low Mach accuracy.
AI.result: The designed schemes are asymptotic preserving (respect transitory nature in limit) and well-balanced (account for gravitational potential). Numerical case studies corroborate theoretical assertions about scheme performance.
AI.conclusion: Successfully developed higher-order linearly implicit IMEX Runge-Kutta schemes that are both asymptotic preserving and well-balanced for compressible Euler with anelastic scaling, enabling accurate simulation of low Mach number flows under gravity.

title: Resolvent estimates for the Schrödinger operator with $L^\infty$ electric and magnetic potentials and applications to the local energy decay
summary: We establish resolvent estimates that extend earlier results to a larger class of electric potentials $V\in L^\infty(\mathbb{R}^d;\mathbb{R})$, $d\ge 3$, and magnetic potentials $b\in L^\infty(\mathbb{R}^d;\mathbb{R}^d)$ such that $V(x), b(x)=O_k\left(|x|^{-k}\right)$, $|x|\gg 1$, for every integer $k$. More precisely, we prove estimates for the derivatives of the weighted resolvent of the corresponding magnetic Schrödinger operator, which are uniform with respect to both the spectral parameter and the order of derivation. We also show that these resolvent estimates still hold for the Dirichlet self-adjoint realization of the Schrödinger operator in the exterior of a non-trapping obstacle in $\mathbb{R}^d$, $d\ge 2$, provided the magnetic potential is supposed identically zero. As an application of these resolvent estimates, we obtain the rate of decay of the local energy of solutions to the corresponding wave equation. In particular, we show that for potentials satisfying $|V(x)|+|b(x)|\le Ce^{-c|x|^s}$, $c,C>0$, $0<s<1$, the rate of decay of the local energy is $e^{-c_0t^s}$ with some constant $c_0>0$, where $t\gg 1$ is the time variable.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes resolvent estimates for magnetic Schrödinger operators with potentials decaying faster than any polynomial, extends these to exterior domains with obstacles, and applies them to prove exponential decay rates for wave equation solutions.
AI.motivation: To extend resolvent estimates to a broader class of electric and magnetic potentials that decay faster than any polynomial at infinity, and to apply these estimates to understand decay rates of solutions to wave equations.
AI.method: Proves resolvent estimates for derivatives of the weighted resolvent of magnetic Schrödinger operators, with uniformity in both spectral parameter and order of derivation. Extends results to Dirichlet self-adjoint realizations in exterior domains with non-trapping obstacles when magnetic potential is zero.
AI.result: Establishes resolvent estimates for potentials decaying faster than any polynomial, shows these hold for exterior domains with obstacles, and proves that for potentials decaying like e^{-c|x|^s} (0<s<1), the local energy of wave equation solutions decays as e^{-c₀t^s} for large time t.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully extends resolvent estimates to more general potential classes and applies them to obtain precise decay rates for wave equation solutions, demonstrating connections between potential decay rates and temporal energy decay.

title: CovAngelo: A hybrid quantum-classical computing platform for accurate and scalable drug discovery
summary: We present a computational platform for modeling chemical reactions in complex molecular environments, focused on ligand-protein binding in drug discovery. The platform implements our new quantum-in-quantum-in-classical (QM/QM/MM) multiscale embedding model that integrates molecular dynamics with a quantum-information-enhanced density matrix embedding theory and quantum chemistry solvers, including explicit solvent. Quantum-information metrics are utilized to generate entanglement-consistent orbitals, enabling a high-accuracy description of strongly correlated regions. The framework supports multiple computational backends, including multi-CPU, NVIDIA multi-GPU architectures, and quantum hardware (IQM, IonQ, IBM) integrated under CUDA-Q, and is designed for compatibility with future fault-tolerant quantum systems. The new platform's capabilities are demonstrated by modeling covalent docking of zanubrutinib to Bruton's tyrosine kinase via a Michael addition mechanism, computing the full reaction energy profiles and energy barriers at a reduced computational cost relative to existing methods. As a 2nd-generation anticancer agent, zanubrutinib serves as a proof of concept for covalent inhibitor discovery. Accurate first-principles reaction barrier estimations provided by our method can contribute to reducing false positive and negative rates in drug discovery pipelines. Scalability is validated through benchmarks on GPU clusters, cloud-based CPU infrastructures. We demonstrate integration with quantum devices (up to 20 qubits), alongside resource estimates for fault-tolerant quantum computing, indicating potential speedups of up to 20x. Beyond single reactions, the platform supports the construction of reaction networks in chemical metric space, facilitating ligand screening and systematic exploration of reactive pathways.

AI.tldr: A computational platform for modeling chemical reactions in drug discovery using a novel QM/QM/MM multiscale approach with quantum information theory and quantum hardware integration.
AI.motivation: To develop an accurate computational method for modeling ligand-protein binding reactions in drug discovery that can handle strongly correlated systems while reducing computational costs and improving prediction accuracy.
AI.method: Quantum-in-quantum-in-classical (QM/QM/MM) multiscale embedding model integrating molecular dynamics with quantum-information-enhanced density matrix embedding theory and quantum chemistry solvers. Uses entanglement-consistent orbitals and supports multiple backends (CPU, GPU, quantum hardware).
AI.result: Successfully modeled covalent docking of zanubrutinib to Bruton's tyrosine kinase, computing full reaction energy profiles with reduced computational cost. Demonstrated scalability on GPU clusters and integration with quantum devices (up to 20 qubits), showing potential 20x speedups.
AI.conclusion: The platform provides accurate first-principles reaction barrier estimations that can reduce false positive/negative rates in drug discovery, supports reaction network construction, and shows promise for future fault-tolerant quantum computing applications.

title: Stable Finite-Time Singularity Formation for 3D Navier--Stokes via 5D-Lifted Axisymmetric Reductions
summary: We present a 5D-lifted analytic-profile program for finite-time singularity formation in the 3D incompressible Navier--Stokes equations on the periodic torus $\T^3$. The core of the construction is a stationary rescaled profile $\Ombar$ satisfying a nonlinear elliptic fixed-point equation in an analytically weighted Hilbert space $\Xspace$, together with a computer-assisted Newton--Kantorovich validation based on interval arithmetic. The profile is reconstructed into a nearly self-similar singular evolution and then transferred to $\T^3$ by periodic extension and exact Leray projection. The manuscript is organized in the style of a computer-assisted proof paper, with theorem statements, proof packages, and explicit validation constants for the residual, inverse stability, and Lipschitz bounds.

AI.tldr: Computer-assisted proof of finite-time singularity formation in 3D Navier-Stokes equations using a 5D-lifted analytic-profile program with periodic boundary conditions.
AI.motivation: To rigorously demonstrate finite-time singularity formation in the 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, which has been a major open problem in fluid dynamics and partial differential equations for decades.
AI.method: Constructs a stationary rescaled profile satisfying a nonlinear elliptic fixed-point equation in an analytically weighted Hilbert space, then validates it using computer-assisted Newton-Kantorovich method with interval arithmetic. The profile is reconstructed into a nearly self-similar singular evolution and transferred to the periodic torus via periodic extension and exact Leray projection.
AI.result: Provides a computer-assisted proof of finite-time singularity formation in 3D Navier-Stokes equations on the periodic torus, with explicit validation constants for residual, inverse stability, and Lipschitz bounds.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a rigorous computer-assisted proof establishing the existence of finite-time singularities in 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, organized with theorem statements and proof packages in the style of formal verification.

title: Physics-Informed Synthetic Dataset and Denoising TIE-Reconstructed Phase Maps in Transient Flows Using Deep Learning
summary: High-speed quantitative phase imaging enables non-intrusive visualization of transient compressible gas flows and energetic phenomena. However, phase maps reconstructed via the transport of intensity equation (TIE) suffer from spatially correlated low-frequency artifacts introduced by the inverse Laplacian solver, which obscure meaningful flow structures such as jet plumes, shockwave fronts, and density gradients. Conventional filtering approaches fail because signal and noise occupy overlapping spatial frequency bands, and no paired ground truth exists since every frame represents a physically unique, non-repeatable flow state. We address this by developing a physics-informed synthetic training dataset where clean targets are procedurally generated using physically plausible gas flow morphologies, including compressible jet plumes, turbulent eddy fields, density fronts, periodic air pockets, and expansion fans, and passed through a forward TIE simulation followed by inverse Laplacian reconstruction to produce realistic noisy phase maps. A U-Net-based convolutional denoising network trained solely on this synthetic data is evaluated on real phase maps acquired at 25,000 fps, demonstrating zero-shot generalization to real parallel TIE recordings, with a 13,260% improvement in signal-to-background ratio and 100.8% improvement in jet-region structural sharpness across 20 evaluated frames.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed synthetic training enables U-Net denoising of TIE-reconstructed phase maps for high-speed compressible flow imaging, achieving zero-shot generalization to real data with dramatic improvements in signal quality.
AI.motivation: TIE-based phase imaging for high-speed compressible flows suffers from low-frequency artifacts from inverse Laplacian solver that obscure important flow structures like jet plumes and shockwaves. Conventional filtering fails due to overlapping frequency bands and lack of ground truth for unique, non-repeatable flow states.
AI.method: Develop physics-informed synthetic training dataset with clean targets generated using physically plausible gas flow morphologies (compressible jets, turbulent eddies, density fronts, etc.). Pass through forward TIE simulation and inverse Laplacian reconstruction to produce realistic noisy phase maps. Train U-Net denoising network solely on this synthetic data.
AI.result: U-Net demonstrates zero-shot generalization to real phase maps acquired at 25,000 fps, achieving 13,260% improvement in signal-to-background ratio and 100.8% improvement in jet-region structural sharpness across 20 evaluated frames.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed synthetic training enables effective denoising of TIE-reconstructed phase maps for high-speed compressible flow visualization, overcoming the lack of paired ground truth data through physically realistic simulation and achieving remarkable performance improvements on real experimental data.

title: Long Time Behavior of Stochastic Thin Film Equation
summary: We consider the stochastic thin-film equation with linear deterministic and stochastic Itô perturbations. The existence of nonnegative weak martingale solutions on the semi-axis is established, and their asymptotic behavior as $t \to \infty$ is investigated. It is shown that in square mean the $L^\infty$ norm of the solution converges to the spatial mean value of the initial condition, multiplied by a random factor similar to a geometric Wiener process.

AI.tldr: Existence and asymptotic behavior of nonnegative weak martingale solutions for stochastic thin-film equation with linear perturbations
AI.motivation: To study stochastic thin-film equations with both deterministic and stochastic perturbations, establishing existence of solutions and understanding their long-term behavior
AI.method: Stochastic analysis approach for thin-film equation with linear deterministic and Itô stochastic perturbations, establishing existence of nonnegative weak martingale solutions on semi-axis
AI.result: Existence of nonnegative weak martingale solutions proven; asymptotic analysis shows L∞ norm converges in square mean to spatial mean of initial condition multiplied by random factor resembling geometric Wiener process
AI.conclusion: Stochastic thin-film equations with linear perturbations admit nonnegative weak martingale solutions whose long-term behavior exhibits convergence to scaled initial mean with random multiplicative factor

title: ProxiCBO: A Provably Convergent Consensus-Based Method for Composite Optimization
summary: This paper introduces an interacting-particle optimization method tailored to possibly non-convex composite optimization problems, which arise widely in signal processing. The proposed method, \emph{ProxiCBO}, integrates consensus-based optimization (CBO) with proximal gradient techniques to handle challenging optimization landscapes and exploit the composite structure of the objective function. We establish global convergence guarantees for the continuous-time finite-particle dynamics and develop an alternating update scheme for efficient practical implementation. Simulation results for signal processing tasks, including signal recovery from one-bit quantized measurements and parameter estimation from single-photon lidar data, demonstrate that ProxiCBO outperforms existing proximal gradient methods and CBO methods in terms of both accuracy and particle-efficiency.

AI.tldr: ProxiCBO combines consensus-based optimization with proximal gradient methods for non-convex composite optimization in signal processing, outperforming existing methods in accuracy and particle-efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address non-convex composite optimization problems in signal processing that have challenging landscapes and require exploiting composite structure of objective functions.
AI.method: Integrates consensus-based optimization (CBO) with proximal gradient techniques, establishes global convergence for continuous-time finite-particle dynamics, and develops alternating update scheme for practical implementation.
AI.result: Outperforms existing proximal gradient methods and CBO methods in accuracy and particle-efficiency for signal processing tasks including signal recovery from one-bit quantized measurements and parameter estimation from single-photon lidar data.
AI.conclusion: ProxiCBO effectively handles challenging non-convex composite optimization in signal processing with theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency.

title: On the classification of solutions to a class of $N$-Liouville equations in $\mathbb{R}^N$

AI.tldr: The paper studies weighted Liouville-type equations with N-Laplacian, providing classification results for solutions using a P-function approach that extends beyond complex analysis methods.
AI.motivation: Previous classification results for solutions to weighted Liouville-type equations were limited: when N=2 using complex analysis, and when α=0 using Pohozaev identities and isoperimetric arguments. There was a need for a unified approach that works for all α>-1 when N=2 and extends naturally to higher dimensions N≥3.
AI.method: The authors develop a P-function approach (a PDE-oriented method) that doesn't rely on complex analysis. This approach is applied to classify solutions for all α>-1 when N=2, and then naturally extends to N≥3 for the range -1<α≤0.
AI.result: For N=2, complete classification is achieved for all α>-1. For N≥3, classification is obtained for -1<α≤0, showing that explicit radial solutions are unique in this range. The results reveal that these radial solutions become degenerate for special values α_k>0, suggesting possible existence of non-radial solutions for α>0 (similar to the N=2 case).
AI.conclusion: The P-function approach provides a powerful alternative to complex analysis methods, allowing extension to higher dimensions and broader parameter ranges. The degeneracy of radial solutions at special α_k>0 values hints at potential bifurcation to non-radial solutions for α>0, opening avenues for further research on solution multiplicity in this regime.

title: Space-Clock Elevator: Multi-Stage Orbital Transport via Rotating Tethers and Elliptical Nodes
summary: Rotating space tethers have long been proposed as momentum-exchange devices capable of transporting payloads between orbital regimes without continuous propellant expenditure, offering a potential alternative to conventional propulsion for transfers from low Earth orbit to higher orbits. In this work, we numerically investigate a system of multiple rotating tethers distributed across different orbital radii and coupled through intermediate transfer platforms (elliptical nodes) moving along Keplerian trajectories. We identify families of dynamically consistent configurations in which neighboring tethers achieve near-phase synchronization, enabling coordinated payload exchange without impulsive maneuvers. Based on these results, we introduce the concept of a Space-Clock Elevator: a modular orbital transport architecture in which payloads are transferred sequentially between synchronized rotating tethers via intermediate elliptical nodes. Numerical experiments demonstrate that such synchronized tether networks can support outward payload transport while maintaining bounded tether tension and dynamically stable orbital motion.

AI.tldr: A modular orbital transport system using synchronized rotating tethers and elliptical nodes enables propellant-free payload transfer between orbits.
AI.motivation: To develop an alternative to conventional propulsion for orbital transfers by leveraging momentum-exchange rotating tethers without continuous propellant expenditure.
AI.method: Numerical investigation of multiple rotating tethers distributed across different orbital radii, coupled through intermediate transfer platforms (elliptical nodes) moving along Keplerian trajectories, identifying dynamically consistent configurations with near-phase synchronization.
AI.result: Identified families of dynamically consistent configurations where neighboring tethers achieve near-phase synchronization, enabling coordinated payload exchange without impulsive maneuvers. Numerical experiments show synchronized tether networks can support outward payload transport while maintaining bounded tether tension and dynamically stable orbital motion.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Space-Clock Elevator architecture demonstrates the feasibility of modular orbital transport using synchronized rotating tether networks for propellant-free payload transfer between different orbital regimes.

title: Last-Iterate Convergence of Randomized Kaczmarz and SGD with Greedy Step Size
summary: We study last-iterate convergence of SGD with greedy step size over smooth quadratics in the interpolation regime, a setting which captures the classical Randomized Kaczmarz algorithm as well as other popular iterative linear system solvers. For these methods, we show that the $t$-th iterate attains an $O(1/t^{3/4})$ convergence rate, addressing a question posed by Attia, Schliserman, Sherman, and Koren, who gave an $O(1/t^{1/2})$ guarantee for this setting. In the proof, we introduce the family of stochastic contraction processes, whose behavior can be described by the evolution of a certain deterministic eigenvalue equation, which we analyze via a careful discrete-to-continuous reduction.

AI.tldr: SGD with greedy step size achieves O(1/t^{3/4}) last-iterate convergence for smooth quadratics in interpolation regime, improving previous O(1/t^{1/2}) bound.
AI.motivation: To analyze last-iterate convergence of SGD with greedy step size in interpolation settings, which includes important algorithms like Randomized Kaczmarz and other linear system solvers. Addresses an open question about improving convergence rates.
AI.method: Introduces stochastic contraction processes framework, analyzes behavior through deterministic eigenvalue equation evolution, and uses careful discrete-to-continuous reduction techniques.
AI.result: Proves O(1/t^{3/4}) convergence rate for t-th iterate, improving previous O(1/t^{1/2}) guarantee. The analysis applies to SGD with greedy step size over smooth quadratics in interpolation regime.
AI.conclusion: SGD with greedy step size achieves faster last-iterate convergence than previously known, with O(1/t^{3/4}) rate. The stochastic contraction processes framework provides new analytical tools for studying such optimization methods.

title: Sharp hypocoercive convergence estimates for underdamped Langevin dynamics via the modified $L^2$ method

AI.tldr: The paper establishes an explicit hypocoercive convergence rate for underdamped Langevin dynamics using a modified L² method with gap-shifted corrector.
AI.motivation: To obtain explicit convergence rates for underdamped Langevin dynamics, which is important for understanding sampling algorithms and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics. The authors revisit and improve upon existing methods to get sharper bounds.
AI.method: Modified L² method of Dolbeault-Mouhot-Schmeiser with a gap-shifted corrector A_m = (m - L_o)^{-1}(L_aΠ_v)*, where L_o is the overdamped generator, L_a is the Hamiltonian flow generator, and Π_v averages over velocity.
AI.result: Established explicit hypocoercive L²-convergence rate Λ = (1/6(√(2+K/2m) + √(4+K/2m)))√m. For convex U, this recovers the optimal O(√m) rate.
AI.conclusion: The gap-shifted corrector approach provides explicit convergence rates for underdamped Langevin dynamics, achieving optimal scaling in the convex case and general bounds under Poincaré inequality and Hessian lower bound conditions.

title: Nature-Inspired Hyperuniform Nanohole Patterning for Robust Broadband Absorption Enhancement in Perovskite Solar Cells
summary: Nature-inspired hyperuniform disorder offers a promising route to broadband light trapping in ultrathin perovskite solar cells by avoiding narrowband, illumination-sensitive responses commonly associated with periodic nanophotonic textures. Here, we introduce a nature-inspired ingenious hyperuniform nanohole architecture integrated into the front glass of a planar MAPbI3 perovskite solar cell, serving as a junction-preserving strategy to enhance optical absorption and photovoltaic performance. In comparison with planar and periodic textures, the hyperuniform architecture redistributed incident light across a broader spectrum of in-plane momentum states, strengthened near-interface electromagnetic fields, and improved long-wavelength coupling into the absorber, thereby increasing the effective optical path length without altering the electronically active interfaces. To quantify these effects, we employed a coupled three-dimensional multiphysics framework that integrates finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) optical simulations with drift-diffusion electrical modeling. The optimized design exhibited broadband absorption enhancement, weak polarization dependence, and strong angular tolerance, while suppressing interference-driven spectral oscillations and reducing sensitivity to patterned-layer thickness. Relative to the planar structure, the hyperuniform architecture increased the short-circuit current density from 21.57 to 23.92 mA cm-2 and improved the power conversion efficiency from 21.03% to 23.62%, while maintaining Voc at 1.13 V and preserving a high fill factor of 87.66%. In addition to statistical pattern-invariant performance, stochastic radius-variation analysis indicated a positive enhancement in photocurrent and under fabrication-relevant dimensional disorder.

AI.tldr: Hyperuniform nanohole architecture on front glass enhances perovskite solar cell performance through broadband light trapping without altering electronic interfaces.
AI.motivation: To overcome narrowband, illumination-sensitive responses of periodic nanophotonic textures in ultrathin perovskite solar cells by using nature-inspired hyperuniform disorder for broadband light trapping.
AI.method: Integrated hyperuniform nanohole architecture into front glass of planar MAPbI3 perovskite solar cell. Used coupled 3D multiphysics framework combining FDTD optical simulations with drift-diffusion electrical modeling.
AI.result: Hyperuniform architecture increased Jsc from 21.57 to 23.92 mA cm-2 and PCE from 21.03% to 23.62% while maintaining Voc at 1.13 V and high FF of 87.66%. Showed broadband absorption enhancement, weak polarization dependence, strong angular tolerance, and fabrication tolerance.
AI.conclusion: Hyperuniform nanohole architecture provides effective junction-preserving strategy for enhancing perovskite solar cell performance through improved light management without compromising electronic interfaces, offering advantages over periodic structures.

title: Quantitative Stability and Numerical Resolution of the Moment Measure Problem
summary: The moment measure problem consists in finding a convex function $ψ$ whose moment measure, i.e., the pushforward by $\nabla ψ$ of the measure with density $e^{-ψ(\,\cdot\,)}$, is prescribed. It is highly non-linear and less understood than the related optimal transport problem. We establish a quantitative stability estimate for this problem. This estimate validates, as well as leads us to introduce, an approach to the numerical resolution of the moment measure problem inspired by semi-discrete optimal transport, consisting in approximating the prescribed measure by a finitely supported one. We describe a Newton method for solving the discrete problem thus obtained, and perform numerical experiments, studying the experimental rates of convergence of the approximation beyond the predictions of the stability estimate.

AI.tldr: Quantitative stability estimate for the moment measure problem enables numerical resolution via semi-discrete optimal transport approach with Newton method.
AI.motivation: The moment measure problem is highly non-linear and less understood than optimal transport. There's a need for stability analysis and practical numerical methods to solve this problem.
AI.method: Established quantitative stability estimate, then introduced numerical approach inspired by semi-discrete optimal transport: approximate prescribed measure by finitely supported measure, solve discrete problem using Newton method.
AI.result: Proved stability estimate validates the numerical approach. Performed numerical experiments showing convergence rates beyond theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: The stability estimate enables reliable numerical resolution of moment measure problem via semi-discrete approximation with Newton method, with experimental convergence exceeding theoretical bounds.

title: Bifurcation of Tetrahedral Non-Zonal Flows in the 2D Euler Equations on a Rotating Sphere
summary: We investigate the emergence of finite-amplitude non-zonal flows on the sphere $\mathbb{S}^2$ arising from stationary solutions to the 2D Euler equations. By restricting the Laplace-Beltrami eigenspace to the invariant subspace of the tetrahedral symmetry group $\mathbf{T}$, we bypass the $(2l+1)$-dimensional kernel degeneracy, obtaining a scalar Liapunov-Schmidt reduction. We analyze four distinct physical non-linearities: a polynomial model, the sine-Gordon and sinh-Gordon models, and the exponential (Liouville) model. We explicitly derive the bifurcation parameter via spectral projections, proving that the bifurcation topology (subcritical or supercritical) is not a geometric invariant, but is governed by the parity of the nonlinearity and the mass conservation.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates finite-amplitude non-zonal flows on spheres emerging from stationary Euler solutions, using tetrahedral symmetry to reduce kernel degeneracy and analyzing four nonlinear models.
AI.motivation: To understand how non-zonal flows emerge from stationary solutions of 2D Euler equations on spheres, particularly addressing the degeneracy problem in Laplace-Beltrami eigenspaces and examining different nonlinear models.
AI.method: Restrict Laplace-Beltrami eigenspace to tetrahedral symmetry group T invariant subspace to bypass (2l+1)-dimensional kernel degeneracy, perform scalar Liapunov-Schmidt reduction, analyze four nonlinearities: polynomial, sine-Gordon, sinh-Gordon, and exponential (Liouville) models.
AI.result: Explicit derivation of bifurcation parameter via spectral projections, proof that bifurcation topology (subcritical/supercritical) is not a geometric invariant but governed by nonlinearity parity and mass conservation.
AI.conclusion: The emergence of non-zonal flows depends on specific nonlinearity characteristics rather than geometric properties alone, with parity and conservation laws playing crucial roles in determining bifurcation behavior.

title: Ultrafast ghost Hall states in a 2d altermagnet
summary: Two-dimensional materials that exhibit optically active spin and valley degrees of freedom represent one of the most fascinating -- and potentially most technologically useful -- platforms for the ultrafast interaction of light and matter. Here we show, via the example of Cr$_2$SO, that two dimensional altermagnets host valley states controllable by femtosecond laser light: linearly polarized light pulses excite charge at one of two inequivalent valleys, with which valley charge is excited at determined by the polarization vector direction. This underpins a rich spin and valley physics including: (i) valleytronics $-$ the generation of nearly 100$\%$ spin polarized valley currents, as well as (ii) a "ghost Hall" effect $-$ the ultrafast creation of states in which spin and charge currents are orthogonal without invoking Hall physics. Our findings establish 2d altermagents as a platform providing a new route for the control of spin- and charge currents at ultrafast times.

AI.tldr: 2D altermagnet Cr₂SO enables ultrafast valleytronics and "ghost Hall" effects via femtosecond laser control of valley states.
AI.motivation: To explore optically active spin and valley degrees of freedom in 2D materials for ultrafast light-matter interactions and potential technological applications.
AI.method: Using Cr₂SO as an example of 2D altermagnets, studying how linearly polarized femtosecond laser pulses selectively excite charge at specific valleys based on polarization direction.
AI.result: Demonstrated: (i) valleytronics - generation of nearly 100% spin polarized valley currents, and (ii) "ghost Hall" effect - ultrafast creation of states where spin and charge currents are orthogonal without Hall physics.
AI.conclusion: 2D altermagnets provide a new platform for ultrafast control of spin and charge currents, enabling novel valleytronic and spin-based phenomena.

title: Policy Iteration for Stationary Discounted Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman Equations: A Viscosity Approach
summary: We study policy iteration (PI) for deterministic infinite-horizon discounted optimal control problems, whose value function is characterized by a stationary Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman (HJB) equation. At the PDE level, PI is fundamentally ill-posed: the improvement step requires pointwise evaluation of $\nabla V$, which is not well defined for viscosity solutions, and thus the associated nonlinear operator cannot be interpreted in a stable functional sense. We develop a monotone semi-discrete formulation for the stationary discounted setting by introducing a space-discrete scheme with artificial viscosity of order $O(h)$. This regularization restores comparison, ensures monotonicity of the discrete operator, and yields a well-defined pointwise policy improvement via discrete gradients. Our analysis reveals a convergence mechanism fundamentally different from the finite-horizon case. For each fixed mesh size $h>0$, we prove that the semi-discrete PI sequence converges monotonically and geometrically to the unique discrete solution, where the contraction is induced by the resolvent structure of the discounted operator. We further establish the sharp vanishing-viscosity estimate $\|V^h - V\|_{L^\infty} \leq C\sqrt{h}$, and derive a quantitative error decomposition that separates policy iteration error from discretization error, exhibiting a nontrivial coupling between iteration count and mesh size. Numerical experiments in nonlinear one and two-dimensional control problems confirm the theoretical predictions, including geometric convergence and the characteristic decay-then-plateau behavior of the total error.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a stable semi-discrete policy iteration method for discounted optimal control problems by introducing artificial viscosity to overcome the ill-posedness of PI at the PDE level, proving geometric convergence and establishing sharp error estimates.
AI.motivation: Policy iteration for infinite-horizon discounted optimal control problems is fundamentally ill-posed at the PDE level because the improvement step requires pointwise evaluation of ∇V, which is not well-defined for viscosity solutions, making the associated nonlinear operator unstable in a functional sense.
AI.method: Develop a monotone semi-discrete formulation by introducing a space-discrete scheme with artificial viscosity of order O(h). This regularization restores comparison, ensures monotonicity of the discrete operator, and enables well-defined pointwise policy improvement via discrete gradients.
AI.result: For each fixed mesh size h>0, the semi-discrete PI sequence converges monotonically and geometrically to the unique discrete solution. Established sharp vanishing-viscosity estimate ‖V^h - V‖_{L^∞} ≤ C√h, and derived quantitative error decomposition separating policy iteration error from discretization error with nontrivial coupling between iteration count and mesh size.
AI.conclusion: The proposed regularization approach successfully overcomes the ill-posedness of policy iteration for stationary discounted control problems, providing a stable numerical framework with proven convergence properties and sharp error estimates, as confirmed by numerical experiments in nonlinear control problems.

title: Asymptotic behavior of solutions to elliptic problems with Robin boundary conditions
summary:
In this paper, we investigate the asymptotic behavior, as $β\to 0$, of positive solutions to the semilinear elliptic Robin problem \begin{equation*} \begin{cases} -Δu = u^p, & \text{in } Ω,\\ u > 0, & \text{in } Ω,\\ \frac{\partial u}{\partial ν} + βu = 0, & \text{on } \partial Ω, \end{cases} \end{equation*} where $p \ge 0$, $β> 0$, and $Ω$ is a bounded smooth domain.
  We will prove that, for all $p\ge0$, the solution $u_β$ behaves like a constant as $β\to0$. However, the value of this constant is strongly influenced by the value of $p$. Indeed, \begin{itemize} \item if $0 \le p < 1$, $u_β$ blows up uniformly in $Ω$ as $β\to 0$. \item if $p=1$ (eigenvalue problem), $u_β$ converge to a constant. \item if $p>1$ $u_β$ converge uniformly to zero. \end{itemize} In the critical and supercritical regime $p \ge \frac{N+2}{N-2}$, the existence of solutions is no longer guaranteed a priori. In this case, when $Ω$ is a ball and $0<β<\frac{2}{p-1}$ we prove the existence of a radial positive solution.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes asymptotic behavior of positive solutions to semilinear elliptic Robin problems as β→0, showing solutions behave like constants with behavior depending on p: blowup for 0≤p<1, convergence to constant for p=1, convergence to zero for p>1.
AI.motivation: To understand how the asymptotic behavior of positive solutions to semilinear elliptic Robin boundary value problems depends on the nonlinearity parameter p as the Robin coefficient β approaches zero, particularly in critical and supercritical regimes where existence is not guaranteed.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis of the semilinear elliptic Robin problem using asymptotic methods, with special attention to the critical case p≥(N+2)/(N-2) where existence is proved for radial positive solutions on balls when 0<β<2/(p-1).
AI.result: For all p≥0, solutions behave like constants as β→0, but the constant's value depends on p: uniform blowup for 0≤p<1, convergence to constant for p=1, uniform convergence to zero for p>1. Existence of radial positive solutions proved for critical/supercritical regimes on balls with specific β constraints.
AI.conclusion: The asymptotic behavior of Robin boundary value problems exhibits a trichotomy based on p, with the Robin coefficient β controlling solution behavior in a way that transitions between blowup, constant convergence, and decay to zero as β→0.

title: Analysis of Log-Weighted Quadrature Domains
summary: This paper studies plane domains satisfying a quadrature identity with respect to the singular weight $ρ_0(w)=|w|^{-2}$. These are referred to as log-weighted quadrature domains (LQDs). The logarithmic singularity at $w=0$ leads to phenomena not present in the classical theory: in particular, when the domain contains the origin, the associated quadrature data are no longer unique, but are determined only up to a point charge at $0$. A generalized Schwarz function characterization of LQDs is established together with a natural formulation of the inverse problem in the singular setting. In the simply connected case, it is shown that a domain is an LQD if and only if the outer factor of its Riemann map extends to the exponential of a rational function. This characterization yields explicit formulae relating the quadrature function and the Riemann map via the Faber transform, thereby extending earlier formulae from the non-singular theory. Several basic classes of LQDs are also covered, and explicit examples are computed.

AI.tldr: The paper studies log-weighted quadrature domains (LQDs) with singular weight |w|⁻², establishing their characterization via generalized Schwarz functions and Riemann maps, showing unique phenomena due to the logarithmic singularity.
AI.motivation: To extend classical quadrature domain theory to include singular logarithmic weights, particularly |w|⁻², which introduces new mathematical phenomena not present in the non-singular case, especially when domains contain the origin.
AI.method: Establishes generalized Schwarz function characterization for LQDs, formulates inverse problem in singular setting, and for simply connected domains shows equivalence between LQDs and outer factors of Riemann maps being exponentials of rational functions.
AI.result: Derives explicit formulae relating quadrature function and Riemann map via Faber transform, extends earlier non-singular formulae, covers basic classes of LQDs, and provides explicit computational examples.
AI.conclusion: Log-weighted quadrature domains exhibit unique phenomena due to the logarithmic singularity, with non-unique quadrature data when containing origin, but admit complete characterization through generalized Schwarz functions and Riemann map properties.

title: Non-Hookean elasticity with arbitrary Poisson's ratios
summary: In a previous paper \cite{Itskov-MoSM} we presented a hyperelastic isotropic material model whose stress-strain response is non-linear even at infinitesimal deformations and cannot thus be linearized. As a result values of Poisson's ratio greater than one half were obtained. In this contribution, we further propose an isotropic strain energy function which is always positive-definite and depending on material constants delivers arbitrary values of Poisson's ratio (except of $-1$) in agreement with the laws of thermodynamics. The model response appears plausible in various deformation states.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an isotropic hyperelastic material model that allows Poisson's ratio values beyond the conventional 0.5 limit, including values greater than 0.5 and approaching -1, while maintaining thermodynamic consistency.
AI.motivation: To develop a hyperelastic material model that overcomes the conventional limitation of Poisson's ratio being restricted to values less than or equal to 0.5, allowing for more flexible material behavior modeling while maintaining physical plausibility and thermodynamic consistency.
AI.method: The authors propose an isotropic strain energy function that is always positive-definite and depends on material constants. This function is designed to deliver arbitrary values of Poisson's ratio (except -1) while remaining in agreement with thermodynamic laws, building upon their previous work on non-linearizable hyperelastic models.
AI.result: The model successfully produces plausible responses in various deformation states and allows for Poisson's ratio values beyond the conventional 0.5 limit, including values greater than 0.5, while maintaining thermodynamic consistency and positive-definite strain energy.
