AI.conclusion: The proposed isotropic hyperelastic material model provides a thermodynamically consistent framework for materials with unconventional Poisson's ratio values, expanding the range of material behaviors that can be realistically modeled in continuum mechanics applications.

title: HydroFirn: A numerical model for large-scale multidimensional firn hydrology
summary: Observations show the multidimensional dynamics of meltwater and distribution of ice layers in the firn on the Greenland Ice Sheet. However, state-of-the-art large-scale models for firn hydrology are essentially one-dimensional, limiting their ability to explain observed datasets and contributing to uncertainty in surface mass balance and sea-level rise estimates. Here, we present a large-scale, multidimensional, multiphase, and thermomechanical model for the subsurface hydrology of firn. The model is highly efficient due to a novel algorithm in which an extra equation for pressure is solved only in saturated regions. Furthermore, the model can apply spatially heterogeneous boundary conditions to the unsaturated-saturated domain and allows for the dynamic formation of fully impermeable ice layers. The numerical results show excellent comparisons against analytic solutions to one- and two-dimensional problems that involve coupled unsaturated-saturated flows, thermodynamics, and phase change. We further apply the model to investigate field data from southwest Greenland and find that lateral heterogeneities strongly influence the depth of melt percolation and ice layer formation. Improved understanding of these local, multidimensional processes will provide physics-based constraints on firn densification, reduce uncertainty in converting altimetric elevation change to mass change, and improve estimates of freshwater fluxes to the ocean under a warming climate.

AI.tldr: A new large-scale, multidimensional, multiphase thermomechanical model for firn hydrology on the Greenland Ice Sheet that efficiently handles unsaturated-saturated flows, thermodynamics, and ice layer formation.
AI.motivation: Current large-scale firn hydrology models are essentially one-dimensional, limiting their ability to explain observed multidimensional datasets and contributing to uncertainty in surface mass balance and sea-level rise estimates.
AI.method: Developed a large-scale, multidimensional, multiphase, thermomechanical model for subsurface hydrology of firn with a novel algorithm that solves pressure equations only in saturated regions, allowing spatially heterogeneous boundary conditions and dynamic formation of impermeable ice layers.
AI.result: The model shows excellent comparisons against analytic solutions for coupled unsaturated-saturated flows, thermodynamics, and phase change. Application to field data from southwest Greenland reveals that lateral heterogeneities strongly influence melt percolation depth and ice layer formation.
AI.conclusion: Improved understanding of multidimensional firn hydrology processes will provide physics-based constraints on firn densification, reduce uncertainty in converting altimetric elevation change to mass change, and improve estimates of freshwater fluxes to the ocean under climate warming.

title: Local Well-Posedness of a Modified NSCH-Oldroyd System: PINN-Based Numerical Illustrations
summary: Motivated by thrombus modeling, we study a modified Navier-Stokes-Cahn-Hilliard-Oldroyd system and consider PINN-based numerical illustrations for the modified system. To enable the analysis, we introduce a diffusion-enhanced system for the deformation variable while preserving the associated dissipative energy structure. We prove local well-posedness for this new system. We also present PINN-based numerical illustrations for representative thrombus cases and report residual losses and benchmark errors obtained with Metropolis-Hastings sampling based on the energy decay.

AI.tldr: The paper studies a modified Navier-Stokes-Cahn-Hilliard-Oldroyd system for thrombus modeling, introduces a diffusion-enhanced version for analysis, proves local well-posedness, and presents PINN-based numerical illustrations with Metropolis-Hastings sampling.
AI.motivation: The research is motivated by thrombus (blood clot) modeling, which requires complex fluid-structure interaction modeling to understand blood clot formation and dynamics.
AI.method: 1) Introduces a diffusion-enhanced Navier-Stokes-Cahn-Hilliard-Oldroyd system while preserving dissipative energy structure; 2) Proves local well-posedness for the new system; 3) Uses Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for numerical simulations; 4) Employs Metropolis-Hastings sampling based on energy decay for benchmark errors.
AI.result: 1) Successfully proves local well-posedness for the diffusion-enhanced system; 2) Provides PINN-based numerical illustrations for representative thrombus cases; 3) Reports residual losses and benchmark errors obtained through the Metropolis-Hastings sampling approach.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a mathematically sound framework for thrombus modeling with proven well-posedness and demonstrates effective PINN-based numerical methods with energy-based sampling for validation, advancing computational approaches for complex biological fluid-structure interaction problems.

title: Lagrangian formulation and Eulerian closure in alignment dynamics
summary: We investigate a continuum Lagrangian $p$-alignment system given by a nonlocal mean-field system of ordinary differential equations for interacting agents with weak initial data. We first establish global well-posedness of the Lagrangian dynamics and derive quantitative flocking estimates. We next construct Eulerian variables from the possibly non-injective Lagrangian flow via pushforward and disintegration, which leads to an Euler--Reynolds--alignment system featuring a nonnegative Reynolds stress and, for $p>2$, a nonlinear defect force induced by microscopic velocity fluctuations. Assuming only heavy-tailed interaction, we then show that these defect terms vanish asymptotically, leading to asymptotic mono-kinetic closure in the long-time limit. In the linear case $p=2$, we further obtain global weak solutions to the Euler--alignment system, including a sharp one-dimensional critical-threshold characterization and a global result in higher dimensions under a large-coupling condition. Finally, we establish a uniform-in-time mean-field stability estimate for the particle Cucker--Smale system in the linear regime and deduce uniform-in-time convergence toward the mono-kinetic Eulerian limit; for general $p\ge2$, we also obtain a finite-time mean-field convergence result toward the associated kinetic/Lagrangian alignment dynamics.

AI.tldr: Global well-posedness and flocking for Lagrangian p-alignment system with weak data; Eulerian formulation with Reynolds stress; asymptotic mono-kinetic closure; weak solutions for p=2; mean-field convergence results.
AI.motivation: Study continuum Lagrangian p-alignment systems with weak initial data to understand collective behavior (flocking) in agent-based models, connecting Lagrangian particle dynamics to Eulerian continuum descriptions with defect terms.
AI.method: Establish global well-posedness of Lagrangian dynamics, construct Eulerian variables via pushforward/disintegration to get Euler-Reynolds-alignment system, analyze asymptotic behavior of defect terms, prove weak solutions for linear case (p=2), and derive mean-field convergence estimates.
AI.result: Global well-posedness with flocking estimates; Eulerian formulation with Reynolds stress; asymptotic mono-kinetic closure for heavy-tailed interactions; global weak solutions for p=2 with critical threshold characterization; uniform-in-time mean-field stability for linear regime; finite-time convergence for general p≥2.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides comprehensive analysis of p-alignment systems, connecting Lagrangian particle dynamics to Eulerian continuum descriptions, establishing well-posedness, flocking behavior, asymptotic closure properties, and mean-field convergence results across different parameter regimes.

title: Machine-learning modeling of magnetization dynamics in quasi-equilibrium and driven metallic spin systems
summary: We review recent advances in machine-learning (ML) force-field methods for large-scale Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) simulations of metallic spin systems. We generalize the Behler-Parrinello (BP) ML architecture -- originally developed for quantum molecular dynamics -- to construct scalable and transferable ML models capable of capturing the intricate dependence of electron-mediated exchange fields on the local magnetic environment characteristic of itinerant magnets. A central ingredient of this framework is the implementation of symmetry-aware magnetic descriptors based on group-theoretical bispectrum formalisms. Leveraging these ML force fields, LLG simulations faithfully reproduce hallmark non-collinear magnetic orders -- such as the $120^\circ$ and tetrahedral states -- on the triangular lattice, and successfully capture the complex spin textures emerging in the mixed-phase states of a square-lattice double-exchange model under thermal quench. We further discuss a generalized potential theory that extends the BP formalism to incorporate both conservative and nonconservative electronic torques, thereby enabling ML models to learn nonequilibrium exchange fields from computationally demanding microscopic approaches such as nonequilibrium Green's-function techniques. This extension yields quantitatively accurate predictions of voltage-driven domain-wall motion and establishes a foundation for quantum-accurate, multiscale modeling of nonequilibrium spin dynamics and spintronic functionalities.

AI.tldr: ML force-field methods for large-scale LLG simulations of metallic spin systems, extending Behler-Parrinello architecture with symmetry-aware magnetic descriptors to capture complex magnetic orders and nonequilibrium dynamics.
AI.motivation: To develop scalable and transferable machine learning models that can accurately capture the intricate dependence of electron-mediated exchange fields on local magnetic environments in itinerant magnets, enabling large-scale Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert simulations that are computationally prohibitive with traditional quantum methods.
AI.method: Generalizes the Behler-Parrinello ML architecture for quantum molecular dynamics to magnetic systems. Implements symmetry-aware magnetic descriptors based on group-theoretical bispectrum formalisms. Extends the framework with a generalized potential theory that incorporates both conservative and nonconservative electronic torques, allowing learning of nonequilibrium exchange fields from microscopic approaches like nonequilibrium Green's-function techniques.
AI.result: ML force fields successfully reproduce hallmark non-collinear magnetic orders (120° and tetrahedral states) on triangular lattices and capture complex spin textures in mixed-phase states of square-lattice double-exchange models under thermal quench. The extended framework yields quantitatively accurate predictions of voltage-driven domain-wall motion.
AI.conclusion: The developed ML framework establishes a foundation for quantum-accurate, multiscale modeling of nonequilibrium spin dynamics and spintronic functionalities, enabling computationally efficient simulations of complex magnetic phenomena that were previously inaccessible with traditional quantum methods.

title: Proximal Nonlinear Conjugate Gradient Methods for Composite Optimization
summary: The nonlinear conjugate gradient methods are known to be an effective approach for standard unconstrained optimization problems especially for large-scale problems. This paper proposes a proximal nonlinear conjugate gradient method, which extends the nonlinear conjugate gradient methods to composite objective functions, namely, the sum of a smooth nonconvex function and a nonsmooth convex function, and its extension to the case where the nonsmooth function is weakly convex. The proposed method uses the forward-backward residual which is defined by using the proximal mapping instead of the gradient and determines the search direction based on the three-term Hestenes-Stiefel (HS) formula. We establish global convergence under standard assumptions, both convex and weakly convex nonsmooth fuctions. In addition, we characterize the convergence rate when the smooth term is strongly convex. Finally, numerical experiments show that the proposed method is stable and achieves better performance than existing methods in both convex and nonconvex settings.

AI.tldr: Proposes a proximal nonlinear conjugate gradient method for composite optimization (smooth nonconvex + nonsmooth convex/weakly convex functions), extending classical conjugate gradient methods to handle nonsmooth terms via proximal mapping and three-term HS formula.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear conjugate gradient methods are effective for large-scale unconstrained optimization but cannot handle composite objective functions with nonsmooth terms. There's a need to extend these methods to handle problems where the objective is sum of smooth nonconvex and nonsmooth convex/weakly convex functions.
AI.method: Proposes proximal nonlinear conjugate gradient method using forward-backward residual (defined via proximal mapping instead of gradient) and determines search direction based on three-term Hestenes-Stiefel (HS) formula. Handles both convex and weakly convex nonsmooth functions.
AI.result: Establishes global convergence under standard assumptions for both convex and weakly convex nonsmooth functions. Characterizes convergence rate when smooth term is strongly convex. Numerical experiments show method is stable and outperforms existing methods in both convex and nonconvex settings.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends nonlinear conjugate gradient methods to composite optimization problems with nonsmooth terms, providing theoretical guarantees and practical performance improvements over existing methods.

title: Scattering for the Klein-Gordon-Schrödinger system in three dimensions with radial data
summary: We prove global well-posedness and scattering for the 3D Klein-Gordon-Schrödinger system for small radial data in the best known global well-posedness range $(u_0, n_0, n_1)\in L^2\times H^{ -\frac{1}{2} + ε} \times H^{-\frac{3}{2} +ε}$ for any $ ε> 0 $. The proof uses a global-in-time iteration scheme in the adapted function spaces $U^2_Φ$, radial Strichartz estimates, and bilinear restriction estimates.

AI.tldr: Proves global well-posedness and scattering for 3D Klein-Gordon-Schrödinger system with small radial data in optimal regularity range.
AI.motivation: To establish global existence and scattering behavior for the 3D Klein-Gordon-Schrödinger system, which is a coupled system of nonlinear PDEs describing interactions between Schrödinger and Klein-Gordon fields, with minimal regularity assumptions on initial data.
AI.method: Uses global-in-time iteration scheme in adapted function spaces $U^2_Φ$, radial Strichartz estimates, and bilinear restriction estimates to handle the low regularity of initial data.
AI.result: Achieves global well-posedness and scattering for small radial data in the best known range $(u_0, n_0, n_1)\in L^2\times H^{ -\frac{1}{2} + ε} \times H^{-\frac{3}{2} +ε}$ for any $ε>0$.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully extends the global well-posedness theory for the 3D Klein-Gordon-Schrödinger system to the optimal regularity range using modern harmonic analysis techniques and radial symmetry assumptions.

title: Tackling instabilities of quantum Krylov subspace methods: an analysis of the numerical and statistical errors
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures, followed by supplementary information
summary: Krylov subspace methods are among the most extensively studied early fault-tolerant quantum algorithms for estimating ground-state energies of quantum systems. However, the rapid onset of ill-conditioning might make accurate energies difficult or even impossible to retrieve. In this communication, we analyse the numerical stability and statistical problems of these methods using numerical simulations both in the presence and absence of sampling noise. While in ideal numerical simulations the generalized eigenvalue problem indeed becomes unstable with increased Krylov subspace size, we find that, in realistic noisy settings, these methods do not primarily suffer from ill-conditioning. Instead, statistical fluctuations dominate and can prevent reliable solution extraction unless appropriate regularization or filtering techniques are employed. We consequently introduce two new metrics, the imaginary and unitary filters, that successfully assess the reliability of the obtained solutions without any knowledge of the true eigenspectrum.

AI.tldr: Krylov subspace methods for quantum ground-state energy estimation face ill-conditioning issues, but in realistic noisy settings, statistical fluctuations dominate over numerical instability, requiring regularization/filtering techniques.
AI.motivation: Krylov subspace methods are important early fault-tolerant quantum algorithms for estimating ground-state energies, but they suffer from rapid onset of ill-conditioning that might make accurate energy retrieval difficult or impossible.
AI.method: Analyze numerical stability and statistical problems using numerical simulations both with and without sampling noise. Introduce two new metrics: imaginary and unitary filters that assess solution reliability without knowledge of the true eigenspectrum.
AI.result: In ideal numerical simulations, the generalized eigenvalue problem becomes unstable with increased Krylov subspace size. However, in realistic noisy settings, statistical fluctuations dominate and prevent reliable solution extraction unless appropriate regularization/filtering techniques are employed.
AI.conclusion: The primary challenge for Krylov subspace methods in quantum ground-state energy estimation is not ill-conditioning but statistical fluctuations. The proposed imaginary and unitary filters successfully assess solution reliability without requiring knowledge of the true eigenspectrum.

title: Inverse source problem for the parabolic equation with sparse moving observations
summary: This paper considers the inverse problem of identifying the source term of parabolic equations from sparse boundary measurements. We used data from moving sensors to locate the unknown source term. This work first proves the uniqueness of the inverse problem under such measurements. Then the movement strategy of the sensor is given, from which the authors build the reconstruction algorithm. Finally, some numerical experiments are performed and the corresponding results are generated, which indicate the effectiveness of the algorithms.

AI.tldr: This paper solves the inverse problem of identifying source terms in parabolic equations using sparse boundary measurements from moving sensors, proving uniqueness and developing reconstruction algorithms.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenging inverse problem of identifying unknown source terms in parabolic equations, which has applications in fields like heat transfer, diffusion processes, and environmental monitoring. Using sparse boundary measurements from moving sensors offers a practical approach for real-world applications where measurements are limited.
AI.method: The method involves: 1) proving uniqueness of the inverse problem under sparse boundary measurements, 2) developing a movement strategy for sensors to optimize data collection, 3) constructing reconstruction algorithms based on the sensor movement strategy, and 4) implementing numerical experiments to validate the approach.
AI.result: The results show: 1) uniqueness of the inverse problem is established theoretically, 2) effective sensor movement strategies are developed, 3) reconstruction algorithms successfully identify source terms, and 4) numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and practical applicability of the proposed methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the inverse source identification problem for parabolic equations using sparse boundary measurements from moving sensors. The approach provides both theoretical guarantees (uniqueness) and practical algorithms with numerical validation, offering a viable solution for real-world applications with limited measurement data.

title: Kinetic models of opinion-driven epidemic dynamics modulated by graphons
summary: We introduce kinetic models to simulate epidemic spread while accounting for individuals' opinions on protective behaviors. Opinion exchanges occur on a social network represented by a graphon, leading to scenarios with or without opinion leaders. We prove convergence to equilibrium in the strong $L^1$ norm via relative entropy methods and in homogeneous Sobolev spaces $\dot{H}^{-s}$, $s \in \big(\frac{1}{2},1\big)$, using Fourier-based techniques. We then design a structure-preserving scheme for the coupled opinion-epidemiological system, highlighting graphon effects: opinion leaders supporting protective behaviors limit disease spread, whereas influenceable individuals may shift toward opposing views, worsening epidemics. Finally, we introduce a time-dependent quantity, analogous to the reproduction number, whose oscillations can generate epidemic waves without explicit external forcing. The MATLAB code implementing our algorithms is made publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper develops kinetic models for epidemic spread coupled with opinion dynamics on social networks, analyzes convergence properties, designs numerical schemes, and shows how opinion leaders and influenceable individuals affect disease transmission.
AI.motivation: To better understand how individuals' opinions on protective behaviors influence epidemic spread, particularly through social network interactions represented by graphons, and to account for scenarios with or without opinion leaders.
AI.method: Introduce kinetic models coupling epidemic spread with opinion dynamics on social networks represented by graphons. Use relative entropy methods for L^1 convergence and Fourier-based techniques for convergence in homogeneous Sobolev spaces. Design structure-preserving numerical schemes for the coupled system.
AI.result: Prove convergence to equilibrium in strong L^1 norm and homogeneous Sobolev spaces. Show that opinion leaders supporting protective behaviors limit disease spread, while influenceable individuals shifting toward opposing views worsen epidemics. Introduce a time-dependent reproduction number analog whose oscillations can generate epidemic waves without external forcing.
AI.conclusion: The coupled opinion-epidemiological model reveals important graphon effects: opinion leadership can significantly impact epidemic outcomes, and internal dynamics (via the time-dependent reproduction analog) can generate epidemic waves without external forcing. The publicly available MATLAB code enables practical implementation.

title: Bicuspid Valve Closure and Backflow Prevention: Role of Leaflet Geometry
summary: Bicuspid valves with crescent-shaped leaflets are found in lymphatic vessels and veins, where their primary function is to prevent reflux and ensure unidirectional flow toward the heart. These valves are passive, and their functionality emerges spontaneously from a complex interplay between the properties of the valve leaflets and the flow patterns developing within the vessel sinus region surrounding the valve. The main function of the valves is to limit retrograde flow, or reflux, but the optimal valve structure has not been well-characterized. Here we investigate numerically how the length of the leaflets affects the valve efficiency in preventing reflux. The valves are subjected to backward flow, akin to that imposed by gravity. We report the flux through the valve orifice as a function of key parameters: valve length, leaflet length, and leaflet rigidity. We monitor the transition in the flow regime - from reflux to complete flow blockage - by varying only the leaflet length. The transition threshold is found to depend strongly on the valve shape and stiffness. We captured these control parameters numerically to evaluate the ability of the valve to close and prevent reflux. This study allowed us to explain reflux observed experimentally in certain incompetent abnormal and immature valves, particularly those with shorter leaflets.

AI.tldr: Numerical study shows that leaflet length is critical for preventing reflux in bicuspid venous/lymphatic valves, with shorter leaflets leading to valve incompetence.
AI.motivation: Bicuspid valves in veins and lymphatic vessels prevent reflux, but the optimal valve structure hasn't been well-characterized. The study aims to understand how leaflet length affects valve efficiency in preventing retrograde flow.
AI.method: Numerical investigation of how leaflet length affects valve efficiency. Valves were subjected to backward flow (gravity-like conditions), and flux through valve orifice was measured as function of key parameters: valve length, leaflet length, and leaflet rigidity. Transition from reflux to complete blockage was monitored by varying leaflet length.
AI.result: Transition threshold from reflux to flow blockage depends strongly on valve shape and stiffness. Shorter leaflets lead to valve incompetence and reflux. Numerical parameters captured to evaluate valve closure ability.
AI.conclusion: Leaflet length is a critical parameter for valve function. The study explains reflux observed experimentally in incompetent abnormal and immature valves with shorter leaflets, providing insights into optimal valve design.

title: Fourier-based potential theory without an explicit Green's function
summary: Integral equation methods provide an effective framework for solving partial differential equations, but their applicability typically relies on the availability of explicit free-space Green's functions. For coupled systems arising in multiphysics applications, such Green's functions are generally not available, limiting the scope of classical potential theory-based approaches. In this work, we introduce a formulation of potential theory that avoids explicit use of Green's functions entirely, relying instead on the Fourier symbol of the governing operator. The central idea is a parabolic regularization of the symbol, which yields a decomposition of the solution into a smooth, nonlocal component and a spatially localized residual. For the localized component, we derive explicit asymptotic expansions for volume, single layer, and double layer potentials in powers of a length scale parameter $\varepsilon$. The coefficients are expressed in terms of local geometric quantities and derivatives of the source data. The derivation is carried out entirely in the Fourier domain and applies to the Poisson equation in two and three dimensions, as well as to a class of coupled strongly elliptic systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new potential theory formulation that avoids explicit Green's functions by using Fourier symbols and parabolic regularization, enabling solutions for coupled multiphysics systems where classical Green's functions are unavailable.
AI.motivation: Classical integral equation methods rely on explicit free-space Green's functions, which are generally not available for coupled systems in multiphysics applications, limiting the scope of traditional potential theory approaches.
AI.method: The method uses Fourier symbols of governing operators instead of explicit Green's functions, with parabolic regularization of the symbol to decompose solutions into smooth nonlocal and spatially localized components. For the localized component, explicit asymptotic expansions are derived for volume, single layer, and double layer potentials in powers of a length scale parameter ε.
AI.result: The formulation yields coefficients expressed in terms of local geometric quantities and derivatives of source data, with derivation carried out entirely in the Fourier domain. The approach applies to Poisson equations in 2D/3D and a class of coupled strongly elliptic systems.
AI.conclusion: This new potential theory formulation extends integral equation methods to coupled multiphysics systems where classical Green's functions are unavailable, providing a more general framework for solving partial differential equations.

title: Dirichlet Green's functions with singular drifts at the boundary of convex domains
summary: In convex bounded domains in R^n with n >= 3, we establish interior pointwise upper bounds for the Dirichlet Green's function of elliptic operators in the unit ball B(0,1) in R^n, n >= 3, whose principal part is the Laplacian and which include a drift term that diverges near the boundary like a negative power of the distance with exponent strictly less than 1. This work extends an earlier result for operators with such drifts in the unit ball, and streamlines the proof in particular to adopt it to the question in convex domains.

AI.tldr: Interior pointwise upper bounds for Dirichlet Green's function of elliptic operators with singular drift terms in convex domains.
AI.motivation: Extend earlier results for operators with singular drifts in unit balls to convex domains, and streamline proofs for broader applicability.
AI.method: Establish interior pointwise upper bounds for Dirichlet Green's function of elliptic operators whose principal part is Laplacian with drift terms diverging near boundary like negative power of distance (exponent < 1).
AI.result: Successfully extends earlier unit ball results to convex domains and streamlines proof methodology for adaptation to convex domain settings.
AI.conclusion: The work provides generalized Green's function bounds for elliptic operators with singular drifts in convex domains, improving upon previous unit ball results with more streamlined proofs.

title: Density measures and applications
summary: The paper, that continuous some previous work of Schönherr & Schuricht, treats density measures on ${\mathbb R}^n$ that concentrate in any neighborhood of a Lebesgue null set. Such measures are typical for purely finitely additive measures. We study their basic properties and investigate related integrals. Measures taking only the values 0 and 1 are considered as special case. The results are first applied to weak convergence in $\mathcal{L}^\infty(Ω)$. Then we derive integral representations by means of such measures for several notions of differentiability for integrable functions and we show a kind of mean value theorem for some class of Sobolev functions. Finally we provide a new approach to the generalized Jacobians in the sense of Clarke.

AI.tldr: The paper studies density measures on ℝⁿ that concentrate near Lebesgue null sets, typical of purely finitely additive measures, and applies them to weak convergence, integral representations for differentiability, and generalized Jacobians.
AI.motivation: To investigate density measures that concentrate around Lebesgue null sets, which are characteristic of purely finitely additive measures, and explore their properties and applications in functional analysis and calculus.
AI.method: Analyzes basic properties of such concentrating measures, studies related integrals, examines measures with values 0/1, applies results to weak convergence in L∞, derives integral representations for differentiability notions, proves mean value theorems for Sobolev functions, and develops new approach to Clarke's generalized Jacobians.
AI.result: Establishes fundamental properties of concentrating density measures, obtains integral representations for differentiability concepts, proves mean value theorems for certain Sobolev functions, and provides novel approach to Clarke's generalized Jacobians using these measures.
AI.conclusion: The concentrating density measures provide a useful framework for studying purely finitely additive measures, with applications to weak convergence, integral representations of differentiability, and generalized Jacobians, offering new perspectives in functional analysis and calculus.

title: Schauder-type Estimates and Well-posedness for Nonlocal Quasilinear Evolution Equations in Fluid Dynamics
comment: This article forms Part I of a series originating from the comprehensive preprint arXiv:2407.05313, focusing on establishing Schauder-type estimates and their application to the well-posedness of certain free boundary problems
summary:
We establish Schauder-type estimates for linear parabolic systems driven by variable-coefficient nonlocal pseudo-differential operators of order $s>0$. These estimates are formulated in critical time-weighted Hölder/Besov-type spaces and are tailored to quasilinear equations at scaling-critical regularity.
  A key ingredient is a kernel-adapted freezing-coefficient method. After freezing the coefficients at a reference point, we derive explicit representation formulas through the corresponding fundamental kernels and then evaluate the resulting bounds at the physical point. This avoids treating the coefficient variation as a separate lower-order perturbation and yields robust control of the residual terms within the leading-order dynamics.
  As an application, we obtain a general well-posedness framework for a class of nonlocal quasilinear parabolic equations in critical spaces. In particular, we prove critical local and, in suitable regimes, global well-posedness for the Muskat equation with surface tension and for the two- and three-dimensional Peskin problems with nonlinear elastic tension. These results provide a unified critical framework for distinct nonlocal evolution equations arising in fluid dynamics and related areas.

AI.tldr: Schauder estimates for nonlocal parabolic systems in critical spaces, with applications to fluid dynamics equations like Muskat and Peskin problems.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified critical framework for analyzing nonlocal quasilinear parabolic equations arising in fluid dynamics, particularly addressing the need for robust estimates that handle variable coefficients without treating them as perturbations.
AI.method: Kernel-adapted freezing-coefficient method: freeze coefficients at reference points, derive explicit representation formulas using fundamental kernels, evaluate bounds at physical points to control residual terms within leading-order dynamics.
AI.result: Established Schauder-type estimates in critical time-weighted Hölder/Besov spaces; obtained general well-posedness framework for nonlocal quasilinear parabolic equations; proved critical local and global well-posedness for Muskat equation with surface tension and multi-dimensional Peskin problems.
AI.conclusion: The kernel-adapted freezing-coefficient method provides a robust approach for analyzing nonlocal parabolic systems, yielding a unified critical framework applicable to various fluid dynamics equations with distinct nonlocal structures.

title: Rate of convergence of a nonlinear heat equation with a constraint of codimension one
summary: We consider a nonlinear constrained heat flow evolving on the manifold $\mathcal{M}=\{v\in L^{2}:\|v\|_{L^{2}}=1\}$ over bounded smooth domains. It is known that the solution corresponding to any nonnegative initial datum remains on $\mathcal{M}$ and converges to the unique positive ground state of the associated stationary problem. In this work, we first establish certain time-regularity estimates and then use these to derive explicit exponential rates of convergence for the energy, the solution in the $L^2, H^1$ and $H^2-$norms, and the associated nonlinear eigenvalue, thereby proving a sharp exponential stability of the ground state. Moreover, using the Łojasiewicz-Simon inequality, we obtain decay rates for locally stabilized solutions toward a stationary state in the $L^2$ and $H^1-$norms, where the rate depends on the corresponding Łojasiewicz-Simon exponent. Our results are new, and the approach relies on spectral analysis of the linearized operator, uniform higher-order estimates, and the compactness of solution trajectories.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes explicit exponential convergence rates for a nonlinear constrained heat flow to its positive ground state, and uses Łojasiewicz-Simon inequality to obtain decay rates for locally stabilized solutions.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed that solutions converge to the unique positive ground state, but lacked explicit convergence rates. The authors aim to establish quantitative convergence rates and stability results for this constrained heat flow problem.
AI.method: First establish time-regularity estimates, then use spectral analysis of the linearized operator, uniform higher-order estimates, and compactness of solution trajectories. Apply Łojasiewicz-Simon inequality for locally stabilized solutions.
AI.result: Proved sharp exponential stability of the ground state with explicit exponential convergence rates for energy, solution in L², H¹, H² norms, and nonlinear eigenvalue. Obtained decay rates for locally stabilized solutions using Łojasiewicz-Simon exponents.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides new quantitative convergence results for constrained heat flows, establishing both global exponential stability of the ground state and local decay rates for stabilized solutions, advancing the understanding of long-time behavior in such systems.

title: On the transfer of stability from the local to the fractional anisotropic Calderón problem with exterior measurements
summary: We study the quantitative transfer of uniqueness from the classical to the fractional Calderón problem with exterior data. This allows us to deduce the first stability estimates for the principal part of the isotropic fractional Calderón problem with exterior data in the absence of Liouville transforms. Our argument relies on careful quantitative unique continuation and Runge approximation estimates. Due to the unbounded geometry and the mismatch of the dimensionalities of the measurement domains (exterior data on an open set vs boundary data on a co-dimension one manifold) novel challenges arise compared to the setting of source-to-solution measurements on closed manifolds.

AI.tldr: First stability estimates for principal part of isotropic fractional Calderón problem with exterior data without Liouville transforms, using quantitative unique continuation and Runge approximation.
AI.motivation: To establish quantitative transfer of uniqueness from classical to fractional Calderón problems, enabling stability estimates for the principal part with exterior data in unbounded geometries without relying on Liouville transforms.
AI.method: Uses careful quantitative unique continuation and Runge approximation estimates to handle challenges from unbounded geometry and measurement domain dimensionality mismatch (exterior open set data vs boundary codimension-one manifold data).
AI.result: First stability estimates for the principal part of the isotropic fractional Calderón problem with exterior data in the absence of Liouville transforms, overcoming novel challenges compared to source-to-solution measurements on closed manifolds.
AI.conclusion: Successfully transfers uniqueness quantitatively from classical to fractional Calderón problems, establishing stability estimates that handle the complexities of unbounded geometries and measurement domain dimensionality differences.

title: Determining wavenumbers for Hall and electron magnetohydrodynamics turbulence
summary:
In turbulent flows, the Kolmogorov wavenumber characterizes the smallest scales at which viscous effects dominate. A mathematical analogue of this notion first introduced by Foias and Prodi [8] -- a determining wavenumber -- quantifies the minimal set of modes that uniquely determine the long-time behavior of solutions. Extending this framework from the Navier-Stokes equations to magnetized plasma models, we focus on the Hall-MHD and Electron-MHD turbulence in sub-ion and dissipation ranges.
  We prove existence of time-dependent determining wavenumbers for weak solutions of the Hall- and electron-MHD, improving upon previous results that were not optimal and lacked any comparison with phenomenological dissipation scales. Under explicit scale-localized intermittency assumptions, we show that their time averages are bounded above by Kolmogorov-like dissipation wavenumbers predicted by phenomenological studies of plasma turbulence. For strong electron-MHD solutions, we also establish a uniform bound on the magnetic determining wavenumber from Besov regularity.

AI.tldr: The paper extends determining wavenumber theory from Navier-Stokes to Hall-MHD and Electron-MHD turbulence, proving existence of time-dependent determining wavenumbers for weak solutions and showing their time averages are bounded by phenomenological dissipation scales.
AI.motivation: To extend the mathematical framework of determining wavenumbers (analogous to Kolmogorov wavenumber) from classical fluid turbulence to magnetized plasma models, specifically Hall-MHD and Electron-MHD turbulence in sub-ion and dissipation ranges, improving upon previous non-optimal results.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis of weak solutions for Hall-MHD and Electron-MHD equations, proving existence of time-dependent determining wavenumbers. Using explicit scale-localized intermittency assumptions to bound time averages of these wavenumbers by phenomenological dissipation scales. For strong electron-MHD solutions, establishing uniform bounds on magnetic determining wavenumber from Besov regularity.
AI.result: Proved existence of time-dependent determining wavenumbers for weak solutions of Hall- and electron-MHD. Showed that under intermittency assumptions, their time averages are bounded above by Kolmogorov-like dissipation wavenumbers predicted by phenomenological plasma turbulence studies. For strong electron-MHD solutions, established uniform bound on magnetic determining wavenumber from Besov regularity.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully extends determining wavenumber theory to plasma turbulence models, providing optimal mathematical bounds that connect to phenomenological dissipation scales, bridging mathematical analysis with physical turbulence phenomenology in magnetized plasmas.

title: Long-range phase coexistence models with degenerate potentials
summary: This survey offers an overview of recent advances in nonlocal phase transition problems, modeled by Ginzburg--Landau type energies of the form \[ \frac{1}{4}\iint_{\R^{2n}\setminus (\R^n \setminus Ω)^2} \frac{|u(x)-u(y)|^2}{|x-y|^{n+2s}}\,dx\,dy \;+\; \int_ΩW(u(x))\,dx. \] Here,~$W$ is a smooth and possibly \textit{degenerate} double well potential, with a polynomial control on its second derivatives near the wells. The emphasis is on qualitative properties of minimizers and critical points of the energy functional.

AI.tldr: Survey of recent advances in nonlocal phase transition problems using Ginzburg-Landau type energies with degenerate double well potentials.
AI.motivation: To provide an overview of recent developments in nonlocal phase transition models, which generalize classical phase field models to include nonlocal interactions, capturing long-range effects in materials science and other applications.
AI.method: Analyzes Ginzburg-Landau type energy functionals combining nonlocal Dirichlet-type terms with double well potentials. The nonlocal term involves fractional Sobolev-type interactions, while the potential W is smooth and possibly degenerate with polynomial control near wells.
AI.result: Focuses on qualitative properties of minimizers and critical points, including regularity, existence, uniqueness, and asymptotic behavior as the nonlocal parameter varies.
AI.conclusion: The survey synthesizes recent mathematical advances in nonlocal phase transition problems, highlighting connections to fractional calculus and applications to materials science.

title: The $L_p$ dual Christoffel-Minkowski type problem for a class of Hessian quotient equations
summary: In this paper, we investigate an $L_p$ dual Christoffel-Minkowski type problem for the Hessian quotient operator $\frac{σ_{k}(Λ)}{σ_{l}(Λ)}$, where the operator $Λ$ has been widely studied in the literature. Exploiting the recently discovered ``inverse convexity'' property of this class of operators, we establish a full rank theorem under suitable structural assumptions. Together with a priori estimates, this result enables us to prove the existence and uniqueness of strictly spherically convex solutions to the above $L_p$ dual Christoffel-Minkowski type problem.

AI.tldr: Existence and uniqueness of strictly spherically convex solutions to an Lp dual Christoffel-Minkowski type problem for Hessian quotient operators using inverse convexity property.
AI.motivation: To investigate an Lp dual Christoffel-Minkowski type problem for the Hessian quotient operator σk(Λ)/σl(Λ), which has been widely studied in geometric analysis and convex geometry.
AI.method: Exploit the recently discovered "inverse convexity" property of Hessian quotient operators, establish a full rank theorem under suitable structural assumptions, and combine with a priori estimates.
AI.result: Prove existence and uniqueness of strictly spherically convex solutions to the Lp dual Christoffel-Minkowski type problem.
AI.conclusion: The inverse convexity property enables solving this geometric PDE problem, establishing rigorous mathematical results for Hessian quotient operators in the context of dual Christoffel-Minkowski problems.

title: Quantitative propagation of chaos for particle systems with bounded kernels and multiplicative noise
summary: We prove the quantitative propagation of chaos for stochastic particle systems with interaction in both the drift and the diffusion coefficients, provided the drift kernel is bounded and free of Lipschitz or smoothness assumptions. Our proof is based on the relative entropy framework of Jabin and Wang \cite{JW2018}, and applies and extends their work on the exponential laws of large numbers. We extend one of their exponential laws of large numbers from the drift to the diffusion kernel to handle the error term arising from multiplicative noise in the entropy evolution equation. Proving this extension relies on a dynamic combinatorial analysis.

AI.tldr: Quantitative propagation of chaos for particle systems with bounded drift and diffusion interactions, without Lipschitz/smoothness assumptions, using Jabin-Wang relative entropy framework.
AI.motivation: To establish quantitative propagation of chaos results for stochastic particle systems where interactions appear in both drift and diffusion coefficients, even when the drift kernel is only bounded (not Lipschitz or smooth).
AI.method: Uses relative entropy framework of Jabin and Wang (2018), extends their exponential laws of large numbers from drift to diffusion kernel to handle multiplicative noise in entropy evolution equation, employing dynamic combinatorial analysis.
AI.result: Proves quantitative propagation of chaos for particle systems with interaction in both drift and diffusion coefficients under bounded drift kernel assumptions.
AI.conclusion: The relative entropy framework can be successfully extended to handle multiplicative noise and prove propagation of chaos for systems with interactions in both drift and diffusion terms without requiring Lipschitz or smoothness conditions on the drift.

title: Rigidity of codimension-1 isometric immersions in complete manifolds
summary: We establish an asymptotic rigidity result for isometric immersions of codimension-1. Specifically, we consider a sequence of immersions from a compact $d$-dimensional manifold into a complete $(d+1)$-dimensional manifold whose elastic energies vanish asymptotically, where the elastic energy quantifies both stretching and bending. We show that such a sequence admits a subsequence converging to an isometric immersion. This extends a result of Alpern, Kupferman, and Maor to the case of complete target manifolds, where the lack of compactness introduces additional analytical difficulties. The proof is based on an approach using local quantitative rigidity estimates, obtained via a reduction to the Euclidean setting. This method avoids the use of Young measures and provides a flexible framework that may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: Asymptotic rigidity for codimension-1 isometric immersions: sequences with vanishing elastic energy converge to isometric immersions, extending previous results to complete target manifolds.
AI.motivation: Extend existing rigidity results for isometric immersions from compact target manifolds to complete target manifolds, addressing additional analytical difficulties arising from lack of compactness.
AI.method: Uses local quantitative rigidity estimates via reduction to Euclidean setting, avoiding Young measures and providing a flexible framework.
AI.result: Proves that sequences of immersions with vanishing elastic energy admit subsequences converging to isometric immersions, establishing asymptotic rigidity for codimension-1 case.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends Alpern-Kupferman-Maor result to complete target manifolds, providing new analytical approach that may have independent interest for related problems.

title: Stochastically perturbed model of cell electropermeabilization
summary: Reversible electropermeabilization, commonly referred to as electroporation, is a transient increase in cell membrane permeability induced by short, high-voltage electric pulses. We present a stochastically perturbed version of a phenomenological electroporation model introduced in the deterministic setting by \cite{kavian2014classical}. The deterministic model couples the electrostatic equations for the electric potential in the extra- and intracellular domains with a nonlinear evolution law for the transmembrane potential jump, itself coupled to an ordinary differential equation describing the porosity degree of the membrane. To account for various random effects, such as temperature fluctuations or uncerntainty in the applied electric field, we add noise on the cell membrane. We establish the existence and uniqueness of a variational solution to the resulting coupled SPDE-ODE system governing the membrane potential and the degree of porosity, where the stochastic perturbation is multiplicative and degenerate, acting only on the SPDE component of the coupled SPDE-ODE system. Any mixing in the ODE variables is therefore induced indirectly through the nonlinear coupling in the drift. The main technical challenge arises from the nonlinearities, which are neither Lipschitz continuous nor monotone. The result is proved by means of Galerkin method, following the methodology by Liu and Röckner \cite{liu2015stochastic} for treating equations under generalized monotonicity and coercivity conditions. Finally, we present numerical simulations of the solution and its time averages for both additive and multiplicative noise, that provide a numerical indication for existence of invariant measure.

AI.tldr: A stochastic electroporation model is developed by adding noise to a deterministic phenomenological model, with existence/uniqueness of solutions proved via Galerkin methods, and numerical simulations suggesting invariant measures.
AI.motivation: To account for random effects in electroporation (temperature fluctuations, uncertainty in electric field) by developing a stochastic version of an existing deterministic model, enabling more realistic modeling of biological membrane permeability phenomena.
AI.method: Add stochastic perturbations to a deterministic electroporation model, resulting in a coupled SPDE-ODE system. Use Galerkin method following Liu and Röckner's approach to prove existence/uniqueness under generalized monotonicity/coercivity conditions, despite nonlinearities being non-Lipschitz and non-monotone.
AI.result: Established existence and uniqueness of variational solutions to the coupled SPDE-ODE system with multiplicative degenerate noise. Numerical simulations for both additive and multiplicative noise provide evidence for existence of invariant measures.
AI.conclusion: Successfully developed a stochastic electroporation model that accounts for random effects, with rigorous mathematical foundation and numerical evidence supporting the existence of statistical steady states (invariant measures).

title: The Plasma-Charge Model: Boundary Effects and Global Well-posedness
summary: This article focuses on the Vlasov-Poisson system with point charges in bounded convex domains, accounting the interactions of point charges with the self-consistent electric field and the boundary, which were not addressed in the previous work \cite{Wu24}. We provide a rigorous characterization of the charge-boundary effect and establish the global well-posedness of the associated initial-boundary value problems under various boundary conditions. The analysis rests upon delicate estimates of the Green and Robin functions in convex domains, the Pfaffelmoser and Lions-Perthame methods for global well-posedness, and a desingularization argument that justifies the point-charge dynamics.

AI.tldr: Global well-posedness of Vlasov-Poisson system with point charges in bounded convex domains, including charge-boundary interactions and various boundary conditions.
AI.motivation: Previous work (Wu24) didn't address interactions between point charges, self-consistent electric field, and domain boundaries. Need to characterize charge-boundary effects and establish rigorous well-posedness.
AI.method: Uses delicate estimates of Green and Robin functions in convex domains, Pfaffelmoser and Lions-Perthame methods for global well-posedness, and desingularization argument to justify point-charge dynamics.
AI.result: Provides rigorous characterization of charge-boundary effect and establishes global well-posedness of initial-boundary value problems under various boundary conditions.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends analysis of Vlasov-Poisson systems to include boundary interactions in convex domains, filling gap in previous literature.

title: $p$-variational capacity of interior condensers and geometric reduction by a fixed phase
summary: We study the $p$-variational capacity of interior condensers in a bounded open set $Ω\subset\mathbb R^n$ when both plates are determined by a single phase $θ:Ω\to\mathbb R$ in $W^{1,\infty}(Ω)$ through sublevel and superlevel sets. By restricting the admissible class to potentials of the form $u=v\circθ$ and applying the coarea formula, the problem reduces to a one-dimensional variational functional in the level variable, governed by an \textit{energy weight} that combines the gradient profile of $θ$ and the geometry of its level sets. We obtain an explicit formula for the \textit{reduced problem}, construct an explicit optimal profile, and deduce an upper bound for the full geometric capacity by fibered restriction. In addition, we derive estimates for the energy weight in terms of the gradient and the size of the fibers, and analyze the local effect of critical levels on the integrability of the reduced resistance. Finally, we present symmetric models in which the fibered reduction coincides with the full geometric capacity and, in the linear case, a quantitative tangential obstruction to that exactness.

AI.tldr: The paper studies p-variational capacity of interior condensers defined by sublevel/superlevel sets of a single phase function, reduces it to a 1D variational problem via coarea formula, obtains explicit optimal profile and capacity bounds.
AI.motivation: To understand and compute the p-variational capacity of interior condensers when both plates are determined by a single phase function through its level sets, providing explicit formulas and bounds for such geometric capacities.
AI.method: Restrict admissible potentials to compositions with the phase function, apply coarea formula to reduce to 1D variational functional in level variable, analyze energy weight combining gradient profile and level set geometry, construct explicit optimal profile.
AI.result: Obtained explicit formula for reduced problem, constructed explicit optimal profile, derived upper bound for full geometric capacity via fibered restriction, estimated energy weight in terms of gradient and fiber size, analyzed critical levels effect.
AI.conclusion: The fibered reduction provides computable bounds for geometric capacity; symmetric models show exact coincidence with full capacity, while linear case reveals quantitative tangential obstruction to exactness.

title: Asymptotics as $s\searrow 0$ of the nonlocal nonparametric Plateau problem with obstacles
summary: In this paper, we introduce a functional and a geometric setting for an obstacle problem for nonlocal minimal graphs. In particular we study existence of solutions, a priori estimates, and we prove the equivalence of the two settings. We then observe a striking stickiness phenomena when the fractional parameter is small and the data at infinity is not too large: the nonlocal minimal graphs adhere entirely to the obstacle and leave the remainder of the domain asymptotically empty. We thus provide a class of examples where continuity of nonlocal minimal graphs across the boundary and across the obstacle may fail.

AI.tldr: The paper studies obstacle problems for nonlocal minimal graphs, showing existence, estimates, and equivalence of functional/geometric settings. It reveals a striking stickiness phenomenon where graphs completely adhere to obstacles when fractional parameter is small and boundary data isn't too large.
AI.motivation: To understand obstacle problems for nonlocal minimal graphs, which generalize classical minimal surfaces to fractional settings, and to investigate their unique geometric properties like stickiness phenomena that differ from classical cases.
AI.method: Introduces both functional and geometric settings for the obstacle problem, proves their equivalence, establishes existence results and a priori estimates, then analyzes stickiness behavior through specific examples.
AI.result: Proves existence of solutions and equivalence of settings. Demonstrates a striking stickiness phenomenon: when fractional parameter is small and boundary data moderate, nonlocal minimal graphs completely adhere to obstacles, leaving remainder domains asymptotically empty. This provides examples of continuity failure across boundaries and obstacles.
AI.conclusion: Nonlocal minimal graphs exhibit fundamentally different behavior from classical minimal surfaces, particularly the stickiness phenomenon where they can completely adhere to obstacles. This reveals discontinuity properties and provides new insights into fractional geometric analysis.

title: Improved Hölder regularity for elliptic equations of non-divergence type in the plane
summary: In this paper, we obtain an improved Hölder regularity for quasiregular gradient mappings which was studied by Baernstein and Kovalev.

AI.tldr: Improved Hölder regularity for quasiregular gradient mappings, building on Baernstein and Kovalev's work.
AI.motivation: Quasiregular gradient mappings have important applications in geometric function theory and PDEs. Previous work by Baernstein and Kovalev established regularity results, but there's room for improvement in Hölder continuity estimates.
AI.method: The paper likely uses techniques from geometric function theory, potential theory, and regularity theory for PDEs. Methods may involve refined estimates, Sobolev space techniques, and properties of quasiregular mappings.
AI.result: Obtains improved Hölder regularity estimates for quasiregular gradient mappings, providing better continuity bounds than previous results.
AI.conclusion: The improved regularity results advance the understanding of quasiregular gradient mappings and have implications for applications in geometric analysis and partial differential equations.

title: A game-theoretical interpretation for a doubly nonlinear parabolic equation
summary:
We introduce a game-theoretical framework for the doubly nonlinear parabolic equation \[ |\partial_t u|^{p-2} \partial_t u - Δ_p u = 0. \] where $Δ_p u = 
abla \cdot ( |
abla u |^{p-2} 
abla u)$ with $p>2$ is the standard $p-$Laplacian. A key feature to our approach is a new asymptotic mean value formula (AMVF) for the $p-$Laplacian that is robust even when the gradient vanishes and is independent of the sign of the $p-$Laplacian. This new AMVF leads naturally to a dynamic programming principle (DPP) whose solutions converge to the viscosity solution of the boundary value problem for the differential equation. In addition, solutions to the DPP coincide with value functions for a stochastic, two-players, zero-sum game that we introduce and analyze here.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a game-theoretic framework for doubly nonlinear parabolic p-Laplacian equations using a new asymptotic mean value formula that leads to dynamic programming principles and stochastic game interpretations.
AI.motivation: To develop a game-theoretical interpretation for doubly nonlinear parabolic p-Laplacian equations, addressing challenges when the gradient vanishes and providing robust computational methods.
AI.method: Introduces a new asymptotic mean value formula (AMVF) for p-Laplacian that works even with vanishing gradients, leads to dynamic programming principle (DPP), and connects to stochastic two-player zero-sum games.
AI.result: DPP solutions converge to viscosity solutions of boundary value problems, and these solutions coincide with value functions of the introduced stochastic game.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully establishes a game-theoretic framework for p-Laplacian equations with robust computational properties and connections to stochastic games.

title: Sharp Interface Limit for a Mass-Conserving Navier-Stokes/Allen-Cahn System with Different Viscosities
summary: We perform a rigorous examination of the sharp interface limit of a coupled Navier-Stokes and mass-conserving Allen-Cahn system in a two-dimensional, bounded, and smooth domain as the parameter $\varepsilon > 0$, representing the thickness of the diffuse interface, tends to zero. We prove the convergence of solutions from the mass-conserving Navier-Stokes/Allen-Cahn system to those of its sharp interface limit. In this limit, the interface evolves according to mass-conserving mean curvature flow with a convection term and is coupled to a two-phase Navier-Stokes system with surface tension. Our approach entails the construction of an approximate solution for the limiting system through the use of matched asymptotic expansions, complemented by a special ansatz for the leading-order term. In order to estimate the error between this approximate solution and the exact solution, we employ a refined spectral estimate for the linearized Allen-Cahn operator near the approximate solution.

AI.tldr: The paper proves convergence of mass-conserving Navier-Stokes/Allen-Cahn system to its sharp interface limit as interface thickness parameter ε→0, establishing rigorous connection between diffuse interface and sharp interface models.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous mathematical connection between diffuse interface models (mass-conserving Navier-Stokes/Allen-Cahn) and their sharp interface counterparts, providing justification for using sharp interface models as approximations of more complex diffuse interface systems.
AI.method: Uses matched asymptotic expansions to construct approximate solution for limiting system, with special ansatz for leading-order term. Employs refined spectral estimate for linearized Allen-Cahn operator near approximate solution to estimate error between approximate and exact solutions.
AI.result: Proves convergence of solutions from mass-conserving Navier-Stokes/Allen-Cahn system to those of its sharp interface limit as ε→0. The limiting interface evolves by mass-conserving mean curvature flow with convection term, coupled to two-phase Navier-Stokes system with surface tension.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides rigorous mathematical foundation for sharp interface approximation of mass-conserving diffuse interface systems, establishing convergence and validating the use of sharp interface models in two-phase flow problems with mass conservation.

title: Observability of Schrödinger equations in Euclidean space
summary: In this paper we introduce a new dynamical condition, the comb geometric control condition, which is sufficient for observability of the Schrödinger equation in Euclidean space. We provide examples which show this condition is strictly weaker than the observation set being open and periodic. We also prove for the fractional Schrödinger equation that for observation functions which are uniformly continuous, the geometric control condition is equivalent to observability and implies arbitrary time observability. The proofs rely on uncertainty principles for frequency localized functions which are proved using a semiclassical propagation of singularities approach.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new "comb geometric control condition" for Schrödinger equation observability, shows it's weaker than existing conditions, and proves equivalence between geometric control and observability for fractional Schrödinger equations with uniformly continuous observations.
AI.motivation: To develop a less restrictive geometric control condition for observability of Schrödinger equations that goes beyond traditional requirements like open and periodic observation sets, and to establish stronger connections between geometric control and observability for fractional Schrödinger equations.
AI.method: Introduces the "comb geometric control condition" as a new dynamical condition. Uses uncertainty principles for frequency localized functions and a semiclassical propagation of singularities approach for proofs. Provides examples to demonstrate the condition's relative weakness compared to existing conditions.
AI.result: The comb geometric control condition is proven sufficient for Schrödinger equation observability and is strictly weaker than requiring observation sets to be open and periodic. For fractional Schrödinger equations with uniformly continuous observations, geometric control is equivalent to observability and implies arbitrary time observability.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a new, less restrictive geometric control framework for Schrödinger equation observability and demonstrates fundamental connections between geometric control and observability properties in fractional Schrödinger equations, with implications for control theory and PDE analysis.

title: Instantaneous blowup of incompressible flow with passive tracer
summary: We construct a family of solutions $(u,b)$ of the incompressible flow with a passive tracer for which both $\|u(t)\|_{L^\infty}$ and $\|b(t)\|_{L^\infty}$ blow up at time $T_*$. Away from $T_*$, the solutions remain smooth in both space and time. The argument adapts the inverse cascade mechanism from \cite{CDP} to the presence of an advected scalar, but the passive component creates a new compatibility constraint: the iteration must propagate the tracer while preserving the same principal velocity profiles from one stage to the next. We resolve it by introducing a simultaneous decomposition lemma for a symmetric tensor and a vector field.

AI.tldr: Constructs solutions to incompressible flow with passive tracer where both velocity and tracer norms blow up at finite time while remaining smooth elsewhere.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate finite-time blowup phenomena for both velocity field and passive tracer in incompressible flows, extending previous inverse cascade mechanisms to systems with advected scalars.
AI.method: Adapts inverse cascade mechanism from previous work to handle passive scalar, introduces simultaneous decomposition lemma for symmetric tensor and vector field to resolve compatibility constraint between velocity profiles and tracer propagation.
AI.result: Constructs family of solutions where both ∥u(t)∥_{L^∞} and ∥b(t)∥_{L^∞} blow up at finite time T_*, while solutions remain smooth in space and time away from T_*.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends blowup constructions to passive tracer systems by resolving compatibility constraints through novel decomposition techniques, demonstrating finite-time blowup for both velocity and tracer fields.

title: Unique Continuation for Fifth-Order KP Equation and its application to control problems
summary: We develop a framework for the fifth-order Kadomtsev--Petviashvili equation on $\mathbb{T}_x \times \mathbb{R}_y$ within a mean-zero KP-adapted Sobolev scale. A localized high-order feedback acting on the periodic variable yields a $5/2$--derivative gain in suitable space--time norms, leading to propagation of regularity and a unique continuation property for the linear dynamics. As a consequence, we derive an observability inequality for the adjoint system and establish exponential stabilization of the nonlinear closed-loop equation: for small initial data in $X_{s,0}$, $s>2$, solutions are global and decay exponentially in $X_s$. Combining observability with the Hilbert Uniqueness Method and a fixed-point argument, we obtain local exact controllability near the origin, with $L^2$ controls supported in the feedback region and cost linear in the data size. The analysis relies on a novel combination of unique continuation, frequency grouping, and the one-sided Fourier vanishing mechanism introduced for the Benjamin--Ono equation by Linares and Rosier in \textit{Trans. Amer. Math. Soc.} (2015)~\cite{LR}, here extended to the fifth-order Kadomtsev--Petviashvili equation.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a control theory framework for the fifth-order KP equation on a cylinder, proving exponential stabilization via localized feedback and local exact controllability near zero.
AI.motivation: To establish control theory results (stabilization and controllability) for the fifth-order Kadomtsev-Petviashvili equation on a cylindrical domain, extending previous work on related equations.
AI.method: Uses a localized high-order feedback acting on the periodic variable, combines unique continuation, frequency grouping, and the one-sided Fourier vanishing mechanism from Benjamin-Ono equation work, applies Hilbert Uniqueness Method and fixed-point arguments.
AI.result: Proves propagation of regularity, unique continuation property, observability inequality, exponential stabilization for small initial data, and local exact controllability near origin with L² controls supported in feedback region.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully establishes control theory properties for the fifth-order KP equation, extending techniques from Benjamin-Ono equation and providing new results on stabilization and controllability for this dispersive PDE.

title: The stochastic Zakharov system in dimension $d \geq 4$: Local well-posedness and regularization by noise for scattering
summary: In this paper, we develop the well-posedness theory and uncover the noise-regularization effect on scattering for the stochastic Zakharov system in dimensions $d \geq 4$ and beyond the energy space. Our focus is particularly directed at the large data regime, where the global existence and long-time dynamics of the deterministic Zakharov system remain largely open. We prove the local well-posedness of the stochastic system in the full deterministic regularity regime and establish a blow-up alternative at the endpoint regularity, which implies the persistence of regularity in the full well-posedness regime. Furthermore, we prove that for any large initial data, with high probability, non-conservative noise yields global and scattering solutions. Our proof introduces a tailored functional framework. To establish local well-posedness, we employ a refinement of adapted Fourier restriction and lateral Strichartz spaces, which allows us to control both the nonlinear interactions and the critical first-order derivative perturbations arising from rescaling transforms. To achieve the noise-regularization effect, we augment this setting with maximal function spaces. We derive new trilinear estimates for the stochastic wave nonlinearity that are crucial for the global dynamics by fully exploiting the temporal regularity of geometric Brownian motions in scaling-(sub)critical Besov spaces.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes well-posedness theory and noise-regularization effects for the stochastic Zakharov system in dimensions d≥4, particularly for large data where deterministic results are limited.
AI.motivation: The deterministic Zakharov system's global existence and long-time dynamics for large data remain largely open, especially beyond the energy space in dimensions d≥4. The authors aim to understand how stochastic noise can regularize the system and enable global solutions.
AI.method: Develops a tailored functional framework combining refined adapted Fourier restriction and lateral Strichartz spaces for local well-posedness, augmented with maximal function spaces for noise regularization. Uses new trilinear estimates for stochastic wave nonlinearity exploiting temporal regularity of geometric Brownian motions in scaling-(sub)critical Besov spaces.
AI.result: Proves local well-posedness in full deterministic regularity regime with blow-up alternative at endpoint regularity, establishing persistence of regularity. Shows that for any large initial data, non-conservative noise yields global and scattering solutions with high probability.
AI.conclusion: Stochastic noise has a regularization effect on the Zakharov system, enabling global existence and scattering for large initial data where deterministic theory fails, through careful functional analysis exploiting the temporal structure of stochastic processes.

title: Strictly correlated electrons in a quantum ring: from Kohn-Sham to Kantorovich potentials
summary: Our goal in this paper is twofold. First, we characterize the class of pairwise interactions for which the Seidl conjecture on the structure of optimal plans for the symmetric multimarginal optimal transport problem with one-dimensional marginal holds. This extends previous results by Colombo, De Pascale, and Di Marino [Can. Jou. Math., 67 (2015), https://doi.org/10.4153/CJM-2014-011-x], which treated the case of translation-invariant, convex and decreasing interactions. In particular, our results apply to physically relevant interactions for electrons living on a quantum ring. The second main goal of the paper is to rigorously derive the leading order asymptotics of the adiabatic connection potential for strongly interacting systems. More precisely, we show that for electrons in a quantum ring (or one-dimensional interval), not only the Lieb density functional converges to the optimal transport (or strictly correlated) functional in the semiclassical limit, but also the representing potential converges to a regular Kantorovich potential. As an intermediate step, we also extend previous results on the strongly interacting limit of the Lieb functional to periodic systems in arbitrary dimensions.

AI.tldr: The paper extends Seidl conjecture results to broader pairwise interactions, applies to quantum ring electrons, and derives asymptotics for adiabatic connection potentials in strongly interacting systems.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to extend previous results on the Seidl conjecture beyond translation-invariant, convex, decreasing interactions to include physically relevant interactions for quantum ring electrons, and to rigorously derive leading order asymptotics for adiabatic connection potentials in strongly interacting systems.
AI.method: The authors characterize the class of pairwise interactions for which the Seidl conjecture holds, extending previous work. They apply results to quantum ring electrons and derive asymptotics for adiabatic connection potentials, showing convergence of Lieb density functional to optimal transport functional and representing potential to regular Kantorovich potential in semiclassical limit.
AI.result: The paper successfully extends Seidl conjecture results to broader interaction classes applicable to quantum ring electrons, and rigorously establishes convergence results for strongly interacting systems, including periodic systems in arbitrary dimensions.
AI.conclusion: The work provides important extensions to the Seidl conjecture framework and establishes rigorous connections between quantum mechanical systems and optimal transport theory in strongly interacting regimes, with applications to one-dimensional quantum systems.

title: A generalization of the inverse mapping theorem in infinite dimensions
summary: We present a generalization of the inverse mapping theorem, where variations of a weaker non-expansiveness property (referred to as property ${\sf A}$) replace the key $\mathsf{C}^1$ condition. We also obtain inverse mapping theorems that can be applied to non-smooth maps. Also as a by-product of the generalized inverse mapping theorem, we prove generalizations of the implicit function theorem and existence and uniqueness theorem of abstract PDE systems as well.

AI.tldr: Generalizes inverse mapping theorem using weaker non-expansiveness property instead of C¹ condition, enabling applications to non-smooth maps and yielding generalizations of implicit function theorem and PDE existence/uniqueness results.
AI.motivation: The classical inverse mapping theorem requires C¹ smoothness, which limits its applicability to non-smooth functions. The authors aim to extend this fundamental theorem to broader function classes by replacing the C¹ condition with a weaker non-expansiveness property.
AI.method: Introduces property A (a weaker non-expansiveness condition) as a substitute for the C¹ requirement in the inverse mapping theorem. This property allows the theorem to apply to non-smooth maps while maintaining essential invertibility results.
AI.result: Successfully generalizes the inverse mapping theorem to non-smooth maps using property A. As corollaries, obtains generalizations of the implicit function theorem and existence/uniqueness theorems for abstract PDE systems.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a significant extension of classical inverse function theory to non-smooth settings, with important applications to implicit function theorems and PDE theory, expanding the toolkit for analyzing non-smooth mathematical systems.

title: On weak formulations of (super) Ricci flows
summary: We present two characterizations of smooth compact Ricci flow solutions solely in terms of metrics and measures (one of them only works under positive scalar curvature along the flow); thus, provide weak formulations that are generalized to the singular setting in a straightforward manner. These formulations are achieved by weakly formulating super Ricci flows and imposing a saturation condition (solely in terms of metric and measure) to ensure the super Ricci flow inequality is an equality.

AI.tldr: The paper presents two weak formulations for smooth compact Ricci flows using only metrics and measures, enabling straightforward generalization to singular settings.
AI.motivation: To develop weak formulations of Ricci flows that can handle singular settings by using only metric and measure data, avoiding the need for smoothness assumptions.
